question: You have been assigned to a new project where the work is behind schedule and the team are not working together well. The team is relatively new and conflicts are arising. Team members are blaming each other for the work not done and some people are not showing up to work on the project at all. What will you do next?

options.A: Meet with the functional manager of that area to agree on team members' time requirements.
options.B: Meet with your team to set a team charter and define roles and responsibilities.
options.C: Meet with the project sponsor to gain additional funding for resources who can work together.
options.D: Escalate the issue to the steering committee as soon as possible as a project risk.

explanation: The Standard for Project Management asks that we create a collaborative team environment including team agreements, definitions of roles and responsibilities, formal committees tasked with specific objectives, and standing meetings that regularly review a given topic. PMBOK 7, p.28-29, Creating a Collaborative Team Environment.

question: You have recently completed a project for a new product and have been asked to manage it after its release to keep it relevant in today's marketplace. Half of your existing team are rolling off to other projects and the project budget is nearly depleted. What will you do next?

options.A: Create a business case and a project charter for your project sponsor to approve.
options.B: Retain as many of your current project staff to continue work on the product.
options.C: Raise a change request for your current project and add scope to continue making changes.
options.D: Secure funding for a stable team, then use current research to form a suite of projects to improve the product.

explanation: This question refers to product management. Product management involves the integration of people, data, processes, and business systems to develop or maintain a product or service throughout its life cycle. PMBOK 7, p.17-19, Product Management Considerations.

question: You are delivering a critical change and several key stakeholders in your organization have added seemingly unnecessary steps for you to take with extra approvals, reports, and meetings. They are also restricting the people you need from working on your project in a larger capacity. What will you do next?

options.A: Go directly to the people you need to avoid extra back and forth with the stakeholders.
options.B: Raise a risk in the risk register on the lack of access to sufficient resources.
options.C: Communicate more often with the stakeholders and gain a deeper awareness of their ideas and situation.
options.D: Show your stakeholders the resource assignment matrix and project roles and responsibilities.

explanation: This question refers to the principle of effectively engaging with stakeholders. Stakeholders may add steps or requirements or restrict team members if they are not engaged appropriately. Communication and awareness of their ideas through knowledge sharing and regular meetings, ideally face to face, may help. PMBOK 7, p.32-33, Effectively Engaging with Stakeholders.

question: You are managing a project team made up mostly of men. Recently Jane was hired for her high expertise in the brands that make up your industry. You notice she has been left out of some key meetings and others are overlooking her opinions. You also notice she is paid significantly less than her male colleagues. What will you do next?

options.A: Make steps to update her pay to the same range as the others on your project and ask her opinion and advice specifically during each meeting along with others in your team.
options.B: Take the lower pay as a win for your project as it will help keep costs low, and her colleagues don't need to know.
options.C: Ask Jane to write down her knowledge of the industry just in case she leaves.
options.D: No need to rock the boat — this is just how people work in this industry.

explanation: This question is on integrity and stewardship. We should have respectful engagement of project team members including their compensation, access to opportunity, and fair treatment. PMBOK 7, p.25, Be a Diligent, Respectful, and Caring Steward.

question: During one of your working group meetings a stakeholder raises the concern that the product you are working on will be discontinued in the next 5 months. You are not aware of this and your project budget has already been approved until the scheduled delivery, which is also in 5 months. What will you do next?

options.A: Keep the project going as it is — the discontinued product isn't your responsibility.
options.B: Set a meeting with your project sponsor to share the information and a recommendation to terminate the project.
options.C: Change the scope of the project to meet a different product so your project can remain relevant.
options.D: Ask for additional funding to see if you can improve the product instead of discontinuing it.

explanation: This question is about delivering value, which is the primary motivator for a project. If the project is no longer aligned with the business need or it seems unlikely to provide value, the organization may choose to terminate the effort. PMBOK 7, p.35, Focus on Value.

question: You have collected the requirements for your project and are creating the scope statement and the work breakdown structure. During this process you notice some functionality in your database system that will help a different project that you are aware of in your project management office. What will you do next?

options.A: Place the feature in the other project's backlog and ask them to prioritize it as soon as possible.
options.B: Raise the functionality in your risk register as a threat with the response to mitigate.
options.C: Add the scope to your project and complete it as soon as possible.
options.D: Raise the functionality in your risk register as an opportunity with the response to exploit, and meet with the other project team and sponsor to discuss.

explanation: This question involves systems thinking, which involves taking a holistic view of how project parts interact with each other and external systems. Finding a previously unknown opportunity, the next best step is to make it known and to exploit that opportunity. PMBOK 7, p.39, Respond to System Interactions (Systems Thinking); p.129, Opportunities.

question: You are working on a high-profile project. Several high-ranking senior executives want to take the credit for this initiative while limiting the impact to their own resources. This has led to several conflicts and a scope statement that is unclear, leading to a risk of project failure. What will you do next?

options.A: Create a scope statement yourself to avoid further conflict.
options.B: Facilitate a discussion with the executives as a neutral third party, focusing on agreed project goals.
options.C: Limit your project resources to the executives until they can agree on a way forward.
options.D: Escalate the issue to the project steering committee and ask them to resolve the problem.

explanation: Leadership is not the same as authority. When senior managers suffer conflict over priorities, neutral facilitation helps more than detailed recommendations. Leadership acumen involves focusing a team around agreed goals, generating consensus on the way forward, negotiating, and resolving conflict. PMBOK 7, p.42, Demonstrate Leadership Behaviors.

question: You are managing a project where the majority of the product has been planned up front, but an external system is needed from a vendor in partial pieces where the requirements are unclear. Half of your stakeholders want to use a waterfall approach and the other half are advocating for an agile approach. What will you do next?

options.A: Use an agile methodology — the fixed scope is low risk and you can capture the changes using agile.
options.B: Use a waterfall methodology and ask the team to plan the uncertain scope better.
options.C: Proceed with a hybrid method to start with pre-planned scope but agile ceremonies that promote feedback.
options.D: Use a combination of waterfall and agile to capture all the requirements and keep stakeholders happy.

explanation: Each project is unique and success is based on adapting to the unique context of the project to determine the most appropriate methods. The tailoring approach is iterative in nature, and this project requires a hybrid of waterfall and agile with the option to improve it over time. PMBOK 7, p.44-46, Tailoring.

question: You are working on a project where senior users have been testing the product as parts are released. They have found some missing requirements and defects. Their manager is worried that the project won't deliver a product that is fit for purpose without defects and within the time frame needed. What will you do next?

options.A: Perform some of the testing yourself to ensure a quality product.
options.B: Help every team member focus on quality from correct acceptance criteria to a developed and tested product.
options.C: Limit testing until the very end of the project so you can do it all in one go when the product is completely ready.
options.D: Increase the testing on your project and add more testing resources so no defect goes unnoticed.

explanation: Quality focuses on meeting acceptance criteria for deliverables and satisfying stakeholders' expectations on product requirements. Quality is everyone's responsibility in an adaptive or agile project. PMBOK 7, p.47-49, Build Quality into Processes and Deliverables.

question: You are working through the risk assessment with your team who are having trouble coming up with ideas for risks. You decide to give them some guidance on types of complexity that might affect your new product. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask them to brainstorm qualitative and quantitative risks.
options.B: Ask them to perform the five whys and Ishikawa analysis to find the root cause of a problem.
options.C: Ask them to brainstorm around possible unwanted human behavior, system behavior, ambiguity, and technical innovation impacts.
options.D: Ask them to perform a retrospective to discover what isn't working well and what still puzzles us.

explanation: Complexity is often the result of human behavior, system interactions, technical innovation, uncertainty, and ambiguity. Complexity can be introduced by events or conditions that affect value, scope, stakeholders, risk, and more. PMBOK 7, p.50-51, Navigate Complexity.

question: You are working on a project where you discover a negative risk with a potential impact of $2 million. You meet with your project stakeholders who determine that this is within their risk appetite, provided that you meet a risk threshold of 10%. What will you do next?

options.A: Note the acceptable risk level as a threat of $200,000 which is 10%
options.B: Note the acceptable risk level as a threat of $1.8 million to $2.2 million which is a 10% variance
options.C: Note the impact as outside the acceptable risk level of $2 million
options.D: Note the impact as an opportunity of $200,000 which is 10%

explanation: Risk appetite describes the level of uncertainty or risk stakeholders are willing to accept. Risk threshold is the measure of acceptable variation around an objective that reflects the risk appetite. In this scenario, a 10% variation around $2 million means an acceptable range of $1.8 million to $2.2 million. (PMBOK 7th Edition, page 54, under Risk.)

question: A risk has been raised in your project around insufficient building materials. One risk owner assigned to the functional manager suggests using a brand new material which has not been fully tested before in a building project. Half of your stakeholder group are happy with this risk response but the other half are not and refuse to sign off on it. What will you do next?

options.A: Suggest a quantitative risk approach to give a more accurate financial impact
options.B: Suggest they proceed with the proposed risk response and ensure it is followed through
options.C: Suggest a different risk owner as an executive may have more power to respond to the risk
options.D: Suggest a different risk response as they should be timely, cost effective, realistic, and agreed to by relevant stakeholders

explanation: Risk responses should be appropriate and timely to the significance of the risk, cost-effective, realistic within the project context, agreed to by relevant stakeholders, and owned by a responsible person. Since 50% of stakeholders are not agreeing, the current response does not meet these criteria. (PMBOK 7th Edition, page 54, under Risk.)

question: Your project is starting to go off track due to a large amount of uncertainty in the requirements and resources. At a significant cost, you managed to reign in these issues. However, your project management office would like you to brainstorm ways to improve with your team so that it does not happen again. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask your team to increase the project contingency reserve to allow for these impacts
options.B: Ask your team to change the project methodology from waterfall to Agile
options.C: Ask your team to use short feedback loops, continuous improvement, and transparent planning
options.D: Ask your team to check their work twice before sending it to the next part of the process

explanation: A project team needs to embrace adaptability and resiliency with methods such as using short feedback loops, continuous learning and improvement, regular inspection and adaptation of project work, diverse project teams, transparent planning, and using prototypes. (PMBOK 7th Edition, page 56, under Embrace Adaptability and Resiliency.)

question: As your project progresses, many of the business stakeholders are starting to voice their concerns, question some of the changes, and even miss key meetings while trying to sabotage your efforts. What will you do next?

options.A: Communicate the vision and benefits for the change clearly, along with the impact to their work
options.B: Create an impact over influence chart to determine who should be engaged appropriately
options.C: Escalate the matter to your project sponsor and arrange for different stakeholders
options.D: Map the current state and the proposed future state of the change for the stakeholders

explanation: This question is on change management, which is a structured approach to transitioning groups to a future state. Too much change or a lack of understanding can lead to resistance and change fatigue. Methods include communicating the vision, goals, and benefits of the change early, along with the impact to work processes. (PMBOK 7th Edition, page 59, under Enable Change to Achieve the Envisioned Future State.)

question: You are analyzing your stakeholders through an impact over influence chart and have found more than 100 different groups of people and over 3,000 impacted stakeholders. Other parts of your project are starting to fall behind as you try to keep up with the large amount of people. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask your project team to engage different groups of stakeholders so everyone is covered
options.B: Clearly prioritize the stakeholders, then engage and communicate with the highly impacted and influential groups early and often
options.C: Monitor the stakeholders for signs of stress and respond to the groups most stressed about the project
options.D: Create a SharePoint page for the changes that any stakeholder can gather information from anytime

explanation: The stakeholder engagement process includes: identify, understand and analyze, prioritize, engage, and monitor stakeholders. Where you have too many to engage effectively, prioritization is the key. Impact and influence are two ways to prioritize, as well as power, beliefs, expectations, and proximity to the project. (PMBOK 7th Edition, pages 11-12, under Stakeholder Engagement — Prioritize and Engage.)

question: You are managing a project which communicates to the customer using mostly pull communication. When monitoring the engagement of your stakeholders, you notice a trend that they are not clear on the impacts or benefits of the project. You decide to add push communication to your communication plan. What will you do next?

options.A: Create a web page using SharePoint with all the project information that your stakeholders can see
options.B: Push more funding for your project and communicate this to the project sponsor
options.C: Push your stakeholders to engage in more two-way communication
options.D: Send a weekly email to stakeholders with a project update including the impacts and benefits

explanation: Push communication is sent to stakeholders using memos, emails, status reports, voicemail, and more. Pull communication is something a customer can pull at any time from a bulletin, web page, whiteboard, or anything visual. Sending a weekly email is an example of pushing information out to stakeholders. (PMBOK 7th Edition, page 13, under Types of Communication.)

question: You have taken over on a project with a vendor where scope and requirements have been missed. You notice that miscommunication and mixed messages are common. You set a meeting with your team and the vendor team. The meeting is extremely important to get right. What will you do next?

options.A: Speak slowly and clearly with the vendor team at a louder volume if necessary
options.B: Confirm they hear your message, determine if they agree, and identify nuanced or unintended messages that they may have received
options.C: Only communicate with the vendor in writing so you have a trail of proof if anything goes wrong in the future
options.D: Prepare a claim for the mishandled scope of your project through the procurement team

explanation: With all forms of communication, quick feedback loops provide the information you need by confirming they heard the message, determining if they agree, and identifying any nuances or additions to the message that they may have added or heard themselves. (PMBOK 7th Edition, page 13, under Stakeholder Engagement — Engage in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.)

question: Your stakeholders seem to be engaged; however, there are frequent changes to scope and requirements causing delays. Issues are often raised and result in multiple rounds of feedback. You are not sure who or what could be the cause. What will you do next?

options.A: Perform root cause analysis with your project team on the problem to brainstorm ideas
options.B: Raise a risk in the risk register for the project delay and review responses with your team
options.C: Review the issue log, the risk register, and the change log for the most frequent requesters, then update your stakeholder engagement approach for those people
options.D: Ensure your project budget has enough contingency reserves to meet the multiple reviews and delays

explanation: A significant number of changes or modifications to project requirements or scope may indicate stakeholders are not engaged or aligned with the project objectives. A review of the project issue register or risk register can identify challenges associated with individual stakeholders, enabling you to update the stakeholder engagement approach for those people. (PMBOK 7th Edition, page 15, under Checking Outcomes — Stakeholder Performance.)

question: You have been working on a project where the manager leads in a dictatorship style. Over time the team has stopped making suggestions, sick days and mental health days have become more frequent, and the pace of work has slowed. The manager comes to you for help. You suggest a servant leadership style instead. What will they do next?

options.A: Serve the team as much as they can ensuring their every need is met
options.B: Focus on obstacle removal, encouragement, and development opportunities
options.C: Ensure the team are serving other team members and helping each other succeed
options.D: Serve the team new ways to do the work and engage them through additional variety

explanation: Servant leaders allow project teams to self-organize when possible and increase levels of autonomy by passing appropriate decision-making opportunities to project team members. Servant leadership behaviors include obstacle removal, being a diversion shield, and development opportunities to help team members grow and make progress in their careers. (PMBOK 7th Edition, page 18, under Distributed Management and Leadership.)

question: You have been allocated to a new project team with members from different parts of the functional business area. It soon becomes clear that very few of them have worked on a project before. There is a lot of miscommunication and rework occurring amongst the team, which is starting to cause delays. What will you do next?

options.A: Set a daily standup with your team to ensure they update what they completed
options.B: Review the resource assignment matrix with your team to ensure they are doing their job
options.C: Help the team set their vision and objectives, roles and responsibilities, and team operations usually done through a team charter
options.D: Send your team members on training in their roles to increase their capability

explanation: When project teams form across different organizations, more work may be required up front to establish a one-team mindset, ensuring everyone understands how they contribute. Team development such as establishing a vision, clear roles and responsibilities, and a way of working are key. (PMBOK 7th Edition, page 18, under Common Aspects of Team Development.)

question: You have collected the requirements for your project, matched them to project scope, and broken them down into smaller parts and the activities required. In doing this, you notice that the manager of the marketing department has added extra scope that is unrelated to your current project. What will you do next?

options.A: Start an open dialogue with the functional manager, raise the incorrect scope items and their impact
options.B: Override the marketing manager's decision on scope as you are the senior project manager
options.C: Raise a change request for the additional scope and let the Change Control Board decide
options.D: Place the additional scope into the product backlog for the product owner to prioritize

explanation: Without an open dialogue you may not know if the scope was a mistake or previously approved. Positive discourse and courage are key aspects of a winning project team culture, along with support, respect, celebrating success, and transparency. Page 21, project team culture in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your project team are protected by a union agreement. You notice that they don't know how to use the product you're improving, they're slow to respond, and regularly complain about the work. Your project sponsor has had enough and threatens to shut down the project if improvements can't be made. What will you do next?

options.A: Set up a new project with different team members and transfer the project work to that team
options.B: Set a clear project purpose and a vision and pair your team with a customer
options.C: Tell your project team that they will lose their jobs
options.D: Create a project Gantt chart schedule and burndown chart so the work is transparent to all

explanation: This question calls for leadership skills where setting a vision and purpose and showing your team how they're making a difference can increase their intrinsic motivation. Transferring to another team may risk the same result, and threatening your team may embed their behavior further. Page 24-25, leadership skills — establishing a vision and motivation in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: During a key scope meeting, an argument arises. The business manager accuses the technology manager of not delivering to their needs on past projects, and the technology manager accuses the business manager of changing requirements that impact the delivery schedule. The meeting is at risk with the stakeholders being closed off to any further discussion. What will you do next?

options.A: Take the side of the technology manager as the scope that impacts delivery should be controlled
options.B: Take the side of the business manager as we should always deliver to the customer specification
options.C: Facilitate to keep the communication respectful, focus on the issue in the present, and search for alternatives together
options.D: Ensure they both agree to the Change Control process to avoid disagreements in the future

explanation: Not all conflict is negative — how conflict is handled can either lead to more conflict or better decision-making and stronger solutions. The PMBOK Guide notes open and respectful communication, focusing on the issue not the person and the present not the past, and searching for alternatives together. Page 29, conflict management in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working on a project where the project team is completely distributed around the country in multiple sites including working from home. Some of the project team members have not been able to contribute to the project and there are concerns from the program that the project will fall behind. What will you do next?

options.A: Raise a request to the program to collocate all your project team members as soon as possible
options.B: Proceed with daily stand-ups and working group meetings with any team members in your area
options.C: Set team leaders for each area around the country to report back to you as the centralized project leader
options.D: Use technology to maintain ongoing contact with audio and video for meetings and a team website to keep all project information available

explanation: For distributed project teams, you can use audio and video capabilities for meetings, use technology including messaging for ongoing contact, build in time to get to know remote team members, and have an open project site for open information and transparency. Page 30, distributed project teams in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are initiating a project to a sales team where the process will change significantly even though the outcome of a completed sale will remain the same. The functional manager is concerned about the change and any disruption that it might bring. What will you do next?

options.A: Kick off the project with multiple deliveries to deliver small parts and reduce the change impact
options.B: Kick off the project with a single delivery at the end of the project when the process change is complete
options.C: Kick off the project with continuous delivery in mind to ensure fast delivery to the business
options.D: Kick off the project using periodic deliveries, delivering value as it's ready

explanation: This question covers delivery cadence and their various benefits. A process re-engineering project may not have any deliveries until near the end of the project when the new process is rolled out and ready. Page 34, delivery cadence in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You're working on a project where the requirements are clear but the solution ideas are uncertain. The product owner and the technical experts can't be sure which ideas will work in practice and which ones won't. The organization can't afford to get this project wrong as they have previously made mistakes and customers are starting to leave. What will you do next?

options.A: Use an incremental development approach
options.B: Use an iterative development approach
options.C: Use a predictive development approach
options.D: Use a hybrid development approach

explanation: An iterative approach is used when you need to capture ideas that might change while still delivering in one release. Incremental progressively delivers features or increments, predictive plans everything up front, and hybrid is a combination of predictive and adaptive. Page 37, development approaches in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition and page 18 of the Agile Practice Guide under characteristics of project life cycles.

question: You're working on a new product for your organization where the requirements are complex and are likely to change a few times as the project progresses. The organization also needs usable parts of the product to be released as fast as possible to capture some of the market away from their competitors. What will you do next?

options.A: Use an iterative development approach
options.B: Use a predictive development approach
options.C: Use an adaptive development approach
options.D: Use a hybrid development approach

explanation: Adaptive approaches are useful when requirements are subject to uncertainty and change, a clear vision is established at the start, and initial known requirements are refined or replaced in accordance with user feedback or unexpected events. Page 35, adaptive approach in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: A new project has been kicked off in your organization due to a recent government inquiry into your industry, which found multiple processes with significant regulatory oversight that need to change. The inquiry ordering these changes has given you a due date of 10 months time before you start incurring penalties. What will you do next?

options.A: Proceed with an agile development approach to deliver change as quickly as possible
options.B: Proceed with a hybrid development approach to respond to changes while planning up front
options.C: Proceed with a predictive development approach
options.D: Proceed with an iterative methodology to improve using feedback

explanation: Environments that have significant regulatory oversight may need to use a predictive approach due to the required process documentation and demonstration needs. Page 40, considerations for selecting a development approach in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your organization is moving to an agile way of work. Your project team are strongly opposed to using agile as they've been burned using iterations when executives used the team velocity to try and force the team to work faster instead of keeping a sustainable pace. What will you do next?

options.A: Add extra resources to your team to deliver faster and avoid executives getting involved
options.B: Proceed with a predictive development approach and deal with the consequences later
options.C: Ask your team to work with agile velocity and iterations despite their hesitations
options.D: Suggest the team uses flow-based scheduling with a Kanban board for visual work

explanation: Kanban is one of the largest parts of agile approaches alongside lean, extreme programming, and Scrum. A flow-based scheduling approach does not use sprints, velocity, or life cycles but optimizes deliveries based on resource capacity and minimizes waste such as context switching and handoffs between different people. Page 45, flow-based scheduling in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your team have not worked on projects before and don't know about waterfall, agile, or any of the different models. Your project may also have requirements that are certain or uncertain with varying levels of risk, so you're not sure which approach to use as you begin this new project. What will you do next?

options.A: Use waterfall and plan everything up front so the team have more stability
options.B: Use general phase definitions such as feasibility, design, build, test, deploy, and close
options.C: Use agile to ensure you can respond to change while your team's capability increases
options.D: Don't use any project approach — it doesn't matter as long as the work is getting done

explanation: All projects will go through the general phases of feasibility, design, build, test, deploy, and close. Waterfall projects will do these in sequence once while agile projects will do it multiple times for each feature or increment — the underlying principle remains the same. Page 42, life cycle and phase definitions in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are a project manager in an organization with a directive project management office and are currently in between projects. The PMO asks you to prepare for an upcoming project in the accounting area by meeting with the functional manager there. You have no team, no scope, no requirements, and no timeline as yet. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask the functional manager for their wish list of improvements so you can take these to your PMO
options.B: Create a project management plan so the project scope, budget, and schedule are more clear
options.C: Create a small backlog of work and start your first Sprint, then progressively update the backlog as the project progresses
options.D: Progressively elaborate the vision statement and project Charter to kick off your project and define a coordinated approach

explanation: Before initiating a project of any sort, we start with a vision statement, project Charter, or business case in order to identify a coordinated path to achieve the desired outcomes. Page 52 under planning overview in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working on a project where part of the project delivers a high value, high-risk component and another part of the project delivers a routine change to the system. Your project sponsor wants to gain the business value quickly, but your project team want to start with the work that they are familiar with first. What will you do next?

options.A: Prioritize the higher risk items at the end of the project
options.B: Prioritize the high-risk items at the start of the project
options.C: Use an incremental approach and deliver part of the routine and high-risk work in each Sprint
options.D: Plan out the work in detail and secure a scope baseline with formal change controls to reduce the uncertainty

explanation: This question refers to the concept of last responsible moment. Work that is novel or risky can be prioritized at the start of the project to reduce uncertainty with the project scope before a large investment has been made. Routine work can be delayed until the further cost of further delay would exceed the benefit. Page 54 in the PMBOK Guide under delivery, paragraph three, 7th edition.

question: You are working on an agile project where the functional manager you are delivering to only has a high level idea of the system requirements that they will need. The functional manager suggests a work breakdown structure, however your team feel that that is more for a predictive or waterfall delivery approach. What will you do next?

options.A: Use and create a work breakdown structure anyway as it is just a tool
options.B: Use rolling wave planning and keep the work at a high level until you are ready to work on it, and then plan in detail
options.C: Note the high level themes or epics, decompose them into features, and again into user stories
options.D: Ask the functional area for more detailed requirements before the project begins

explanation: This question refers to decomposition, or breaking down the work. We could use a work breakdown structure or rolling wave planning, but for an incremental approach, breaking down the work from epics into features to user stories is a form of decomposition that fits the delivery approach we are using. Page 54 under delivery, paragraph 3, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are at the beginning of planning a new project and have researched and gathered the requirements from the area, broken them down into deliverables and activities, which you believe could take 26 weeks to deliver. The project sponsor is asking you for an estimate on how long the project will take. What will you do next?

options.A: Tell the project sponsor 26 weeks
options.B: Tell the project sponsor that you will use an iterative approach where value is delivered until the project stops
options.C: Tell the project sponsor approximately 24 to 28 weeks
options.D: Tell the project sponsor approximately 20 to 45 weeks

explanation: This question is related to estimation and range estimates should have a broad range at the start of the project when there is not much information, starting at approximately minus 25 to plus 75 percent. Once the project team has a smooth delivery cadence and experience in the product, a smaller range such as minus 5 to plus 10 percent can be used. Page 55 under estimating and range in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: After gathering your project requirements, project scope, and breaking it down into activities, you need to estimate the project schedule with your team. After some analysis and discussion your team come up with a range of 53 to 75 days assigned across all tasks. What will you do next?

options.A: Low accuracy and low precision; ensure estimates become more accurate as the project unfolds
options.B: Low accuracy and high precision; ensure the estimates become more accurate as the project unfolds
options.C: High confidence and high precision; ensure the estimates become more accurate as the project unfolds
options.D: High confidence and low precision; ensure the estimates become more accurate as the project unfolds

explanation: This question is on estimates and their accuracy. The lower the accuracy, the larger the potential range in values, which is what we see with the wide 53 to 75 day range. Precision is different from accuracy as it refers to the exactness of the estimate; for example, 2 days is more precise than sometime this week. Page 54 in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition under estimating, accuracy, precision, and confidence.

question: You are working on a business case where you know that the industry is undergoing a significant change at the moment and there could be many different impacts to your project benefits before it is delivered completely. Your project sponsor asks you for the best estimate of benefits that you can give under the circumstances. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask your team for a probabilistic estimate
options.B: Ask your team for a deterministic estimate
options.C: Ask your team for an absolute estimate
options.D: Ask your team for a relative estimate

explanation: Probabilistic estimates include a range of estimates along with the associated probabilities within the range, such as a confidence level or a probability distribution. When the number is uncertain, this is the best approach. Deterministic is a specific number, absolute is also a specific number, and relative estimates are shown in comparison to other estimates. Page 57 under presenting estimates in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You have noted nine epics for delivery as part of your most recent project. Your team have broken down these epics into user stories and would like to see the effort involved for these deliverables. You ask your team to find the smallest user story, size it with a one, and then determine the size of the other cards based on how they compare to the original card. What approach are you taking?

options.A: Relative estimating with your team
options.B: Deterministic estimating to determine the effort
options.C: Absolute estimating
options.D: Probabilistic estimating

explanation: This question is about estimating and making an estimate in comparison to other estimates, which is relative estimating. Page 57 under relative estimating in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working on a project where a competitor is also working on a similar feature and being first to the market will have a significant impact on this year's profit. Your product owner would like to measure how quickly story cards are being completed once they start being developed. What will you do next?

options.A: Check the lead time
options.B: Check the Gantt chart
options.C: Check the cycle time
options.D: Check the schedule network and precedence diagram

explanation: Cycle time is the total elapsed time it takes one unit to get through a process, meaning from when a developer starts working on a card to when they complete it. Lead time is the time from when the customer makes the order or the requirement is noted to the time that the item is delivered or released. Page 58 under flow-based estimating in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working on a project that has recently moved to a more agile way of work. The team have not completely taken on the Scrum approach however and are comfortable working with a more flow-based approach, moving user stories across a Kanban board. You are wanting to get an estimate on how much work can be completed in the next 3 months based on the team's existing process progress. What will you do next?

options.A: Multiply the velocity by the number of remaining user stories
options.B: Multiply the cycle time by the throughput
options.C: Multiply the lead time by the schedule performance index
options.D: Multiply the planned value by the actual value

explanation: Scrum uses velocity; Kanban or flow-based estimates are developed by determining the cycle time and throughput. Cycle time is the total elapsed time it takes one unit to get through a process, and throughput is the number of items that can complete a process in a given amount of time. Page 58 under flow-based estimating in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your product owner wants to bring the next feature forward so that it is done in parallel to the current feature, but it is still released in the existing feature order. You make the necessary adjustments to the product backlog and decide to adjust the product roadmap which you have visualized as a Gantt chart. What will you do next?

options.A: Use a schedule lead and change the feature relationship to finish to start
options.B: Use a schedule lag and change the feature relationship to finish to start
options.C: Use a schedule lead and change the feature relationship to finish to finish
options.D: Use a schedule lag and change the feature relationship to finish to finish

explanation: Schedule lag is moving an item back; schedule lead is bringing an item forward. The precedence diagramming method states that for finish to finish items, the next item cannot finish until the previous item has finished, which allows items to be worked on in parallel while still keeping the same delivery order. Page 59 schedules lead and lag in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition and page 180 precedence diagramming method in the 6th edition.

question: You're working on a project where you have been completing the deliverables in sequence and the project work is starting to fall behind. The project sponsor asks you to speed up the work and reduce the time for the project to be delivered, but also makes it very clear that there is no more money to use when speeding up the work. What will you do next?

options.A: Apply leads and lags to adjust the schedule
options.B: Fast track the schedule by performing activities in parallel
options.C: Use resource leveling to optimize the schedule
options.D: Crash the schedule by adding extra resources

explanation: Fast tracking is a schedule compression method in which activities or tasks that are normally done in sequence are performed in parallel at the same time. Schedule crashing includes adding people to activities, working overtime, or paying to expedite deliveries, so it cannot be used when there is no extra money. See page 59 under schedules fast tracking and crashing in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: After reviewing your project schedule you notice you are behind by 3 weeks and the schedule needs to be compressed. Your project does not have enough budget to crash the schedule. You check with the lead for each area and review the schedule as to whether they can fast track their deliverables. What will you do next?

options.A: Fast track activities with discretionary dependencies
options.B: Fast track activities with industry-regulated dependencies
options.C: Fast track activities with external dependencies
options.D: Fast track activities with mandatory dependencies

explanation: When compressing the schedule, some activities cannot be fast-tracked because of the nature of the work while others can. A discretionary dependency is based on project preferences and might be modifiable. External and mandatory dependencies usually cannot be modified, and industry-regulated dependencies have significant oversight making them difficult to move.

question: Your team are new to Agile and have previously been working with waterfall. Your key customer asks you for a project schedule. The project team believes using a project schedule is from the old waterfall way of working and they should not have to create one. What will you do next?

options.A: Create a project Gantt chart to visualize the activities within the project
options.B: Determine the project's planned versus actual value instead of working with a schedule directly
options.C: Advise the customer that you don't need to plan ahead in an adaptive way of working
options.D: Ask your team to develop a high-level release plan like a product road map showing the features to be included in each release

explanation: Adaptive schedule planning uses incremental planning. A high-level release plan is developed that indicates the basic features and functionality to be included in each release. This satisfies the customer need for visibility while aligning with agile ways of working. See page 61 under adaptive schedule planning in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You're working in a project team that has recently moved to an agile way of work. Your business analysts want to gather the requirements, match solutions to the scope, and do a work breakdown structure for the whole deliverable. Your product owner says this will take too long and is not agile enough for their needs. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask your team to review the scope management plan for the proper process
options.B: Break the deliverable down into smaller pieces so you can deliver those faster
options.C: Ask your team to time box work on the highest priority items in the backlog, update the estimates, and then reprioritize them as necessary
options.D: Review the project charter for the high-level scope and then use this to begin delivery quickly

explanation: Adaptive approaches often use time boxes where the work is based on a prioritized backlog. The project team determines the amount of work they can do in each time box, estimates the work, and self-manages to accomplish that work. At this point the backlog may be reprioritized for the next time box. See page 62 under time boxes in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: When reviewing the current progress of your project with your project sponsor, they notice that the system solution is missing a critical piece that will impact the benefit of the project. The project sponsor approves the scope. Your project has a planned value of $520,000 and an actual cost of $535,000. What will you do next?

options.A: Work with the PMO to unlock the project management reserves for the extra work
options.B: Ask the product owner to reprioritize the backlog and see if the new work will fit
options.C: Perform the work within your normal budget as your project is on track
options.D: Raise a change request for the changes and gain approval from the Change Control Board

explanation: Management reserves are set aside for unexpected activities related to in-scope work. Depending on the organization policies, management reserves may be managed by the PMO at the program or portfolio level. The project is over budget (AC $535K vs PV $520K) and the scope has already been approved by the sponsor, so unlocking management reserves is the correct action. See page 62 under budget in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You're in the planning stages of an agile project where the functional manager and senior users have provided the high-level requirements and scope. You have been asked to put together a project team that will be able to deliver quickly. What will you do next?

options.A: Select project team members from each city to ensure diversity of knowledge within the team
options.B: Put together a resource management plan outlining the resources required
options.C: Ask your PMO for current available resources and assign them to the work
options.D: Select a small team that can work in the same area so they can solve problems as they arise

explanation: When planning for the team, the project sponsor considers the ability and necessity for them to work in the same location. Small project teams that can work in the same room are able to take advantage of osmotic communication, overhearing conversations and solving problems together quickly. See page 64 under project team composition and structure in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You're putting together a project team to deliver an accounting system to offices around the country. The accounting system you're moving to has not been used by anyone in your organization before. The project is quite high risk and the delivery needs to be right the first time. What will you do next?

options.A: Select a small team that can work in the same area so they can solve problems as they arise
options.B: Use a predictive project approach and source part of your team externally
options.C: Train existing internal staff in the new system and then have them work on the project
options.D: Perform a make or buy analysis on the different software options

explanation: The benefit that outside skills bring to the project are weighed against the costs that will be incurred. With higher risk and a single delivery, a waterfall or predictive project delivery approach is best, and sourcing external team members who have expertise in the new system addresses the gap in organizational knowledge. See page 63 under project team composition and structure in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You're putting together a project plan for a change that affects nearly 10,000 stakeholders in your organization. You have identified the affected stakeholders, analyzed and prioritized them, and are putting together a communication plan to ensure that the right engagement is made. What will you NOT include in your plan?

options.A: Why should information be shared with stakeholders
options.B: What is the best way to provide them the information
options.C: How can they make changes to the communication plan
options.D: When and how often is information needed

explanation: Planning communication for the project entails considering: who needs the information, why should information be shared with stakeholders, when and how often is the information needed, what is the best way to provide the information, and what information does each stakeholder need. How stakeholders can make changes to the communication plan is not a standard element included. See page 64 in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You put together a resource plan for a large amount of physical inventory to be purchased from overseas. You ordered the inventory during project execution; however, global supply chains were impacted and the shipment was delayed by 3 months, significantly impacting your project. What should you have done differently?

options.A: Hired a resource manager to take care of the resource tasks on your project
options.B: Planned strategically about the timing from order to delivery to usage, managing resource risks and their responses
options.C: Understood what it means to track the inventory from arrival on site to the delivery of an integrated product
options.D: Sourced the inventory locally to reduce the impact of supply chains

explanation: Project teams whose projects require significant physical materials should think and plan strategically about the timing from order to delivery to usage. This can include evaluation of bulk ordering versus the cost of storage, global logistics, and more. The supply chain delay was a risk that should have been identified and managed with appropriate responses. See page 65 under physical resources in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You are halfway through your agile project when there is a significant change to the project scope after it was found that the project would not be able to deliver the benefits that it had promised. You check your project budget and there are contingency and management reserves available. What will you do next?

options.A: Take the project scope change to the Change Control Board for approval
options.B: Use the available contingency reserves to make the appropriate change to the project scope
options.C: Review the change management plan for the Change Control process
options.D: Reprioritize the backlog including the new scope and begin work on the highest priority item

explanation: Changes may occur as a result of a risk event, environment change, a deeper understanding of requirements, customer requests, and more. In an agile team, this may take the form of reprioritizing the backlog instead of a formal Change Control process with project baselines. Contingency reserves are for project risks, not scope changes, making backlog reprioritization the correct agile approach. See page 66 under changes in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You have recently taken over on a project that is behind schedule and slightly over budget. You raise an issue at the steering committee meeting where they advise that there is no additional funding available for the project and the project must finish on time. You believe that you can optimize the project process to bring it back on track. What will you do next?

options.A: Add additional people and resources to the project to speed it up
options.B: Utilize the project's contingency reserve to source the resources you need
options.C: Perform project activities in parallel, completing multiple items at once instead of one at a time
options.D: Use lean production methods to reduce non-value-added activities and retrospectives for the team to suggest improvements

explanation: Ways of optimizing project processes for the environment include lean production methods, retrospectives or Lessons Learned, and finding where the next best funding is spent. Adding people and resources may not always improve things; it can also increase the complexity and the communication channels on your project. This is from page 71 under project processes in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition.

question: Your project management office is optimizing all the projects within their portfolio. They ask for your advice in tracking the type of work each team member is doing and how long it takes, suggesting that they categorize and record their work in addition to the work itself. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask your team to record and categorize their work as it is important
options.B: Recommend an automated reporting tool where possible as categorizing their work is non-value-added
options.C: Recommend an automated reporting tool because recording their work is value-added
options.D: Ask your team to ignore the request and focus on delivering customer value only

explanation: The time taken to track or record time can be viewed as non-value-added work that does not directly benefit the output or the receiving customer. We should respect the PMO request but in addition to being efficient, processes should also be effective. This is from page 72 under project processes in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition.

question: You are working on an agile project as a Scrum Master where the functional manager has worked with some people in your development team before. She approaches them directly to see if they can add a feature to the product in their current Sprint. What will you do next?

options.A: Adjust the Sprint backlog to allow for the additional feature
options.B: Adjust the product backlog to add the customer requirement
options.C: Ask the manager to speak with the product owner for the product priorities, then protect the team from direct requests in the future
options.D: Ask the manager to create a change request so it can go through the proper process

explanation: Project managers have a responsibility for assessing and balancing project team focus and attention. This may involve protecting the team's production capability, their team health and satisfaction, and protecting them from outside influences. In agile, this also means being a diversion shield. This is from page 73 under maintaining project team focus in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition.

question: Part of the project management plan is a detailed communications plan which has the stakeholders on the project, their communication needs, and how information will be communicated. While this is being signed off by the project sponsor, you receive many ad hoc requests for information from new stakeholders. What will you do next?

options.A: Review the communications management plan to include the stakeholders with the information that they need and their communication preferences
options.B: Create an information radiator and ask the stakeholders to pull the information they need
options.C: Ignore the new requests as you have captured all the stakeholders you need in your plan
options.D: Ask the new stakeholders to review your communications management plan to see how you are distributing information

explanation: Once collected, information is distributed as indicated in the project management communications plan. An abundance of ad hoc communication requests may indicate that the communications planning was not sufficient to meet stakeholder needs and it is a good idea to review it. This is from page 72 under project communication and engagement in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition.

question: You are working on a high-value construction project to include smart home components within multi-million dollar buildings. After completing a make or buy analysis, you find that it will cost $700,000 to make a new system or $500,000 to buy it from a vendor. You prepare a procurement management plan to purchase it. What will you do before conducting a procurement?

options.A: Work with your project team to complete as much as possible in-house
options.B: Work with contracting professionals in your organization to develop an RFP, statement of work, and terms and conditions
options.C: Adjust your stakeholder register to include the new vendor and add the scope to your work breakdown structure dictionary
options.D: Work with your project team to develop an RMP, SMP, and list of resources

explanation: Prior to conducting a procurement, the project manager and technically qualified project team members work with contracting professionals to develop the request for proposal, statement of work, terms and conditions, and other necessary documents to go out to bid. This is from page 74 under working with procurements in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition.

question: A missing item has been uncovered in the project scope that your project team cannot deliver. The item is not complex and seems to be fairly readily available from various sources. You are ready to move to source selection and choose a vendor that will meet the needs. What will you do before sending out those bid documents to vendors?

options.A: Prepare a request for proposal for vendors to provide a solution to your needs
options.B: Prepare a request for quote to determine the best price from the vendors
options.C: Prepare a request for information to gather more information and viable sources from the market
options.D: Review the procurement management plan for your project's procurement process

explanation: The request for information is used to gather more information from the market prior to sending out bid documents to a set of selected vendors. A request for proposal is used where scope is complex or the buyer is looking for the vendor to provide a solution. A request for quote is when price is the deciding factor. This is from page 75 in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition.

question: You are working on a project where part of the work is planned carefully up front and another part is subject to change. You negotiate terms and conditions including cost, delivery, and payment dates. The vendor is concerned about confusion that might occur between pre-planned and changeable work. What will you do next?

options.A: Use a firm fixed price contract to ensure the cost is fixed but the scope can remain flexible
options.B: Use a time and materials contract to ensure only the time and the materials used are paid for
options.C: Adjust your project plan to suit either a predictive or adaptive approach to reduce the confusion
options.D: Use a master agreement for the overall contract with the adaptive work placed in an appendix

explanation: For projects that use an adaptive approach for some deliverables and a predictive approach for others, a master agreement is used and the adaptive work may be placed in an appendix or a supplement. This allows the changes to occur on the adaptive scope without impacting the overall contract. This is from page 76 under the bid process contracting in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition.

question: You are working in an agile project where there are vastly differing levels of product knowledge within your team. The Scrum Master suggests that you try to gain knowledge by osmosis because the project information may be lost when only communicating by email. What will you do next?

options.A: Collocate the team to capture the benefit of each person's tacit knowledge
options.B: Refer to the organizational process assets to capture the team's explicit knowledge
options.C: Refer to the project's knowledge management system and capture the project's explicit knowledge
options.D: Create a process to capture the team's tacit knowledge

explanation: Learning by osmosis is capturing information from immediate surroundings such as collocating the team. Tacit knowledge is information that is not straightforward, such as experience, insights, and practical knowledge or skill. Explicit knowledge can be readily codified using words, a process, or pictures. This is from page 77 under explicit and tacit knowledge in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition.

question: You are working as a product owner and have placed high-level information about the product in the product backlog with the highest priority feature at the top. Senior users informing the project are unable to clearly articulate their requirements and are concerned that the product will not truly meet their needs. What will you do next?

options.A: Proceed with the highest priority item during the first Sprint
options.B: Work with the customer to create mockups and prototypes to discover what works for them
options.C: Ask your stakeholders to submit a change request and update the project scope accordingly
options.D: Begin breaking down the work into user stories to place into the Sprint

explanation: On projects that do not have clearly defined requirements, prototypes, demonstrations, storyboards, and mockups can be used to evolve those requirements. In these situations, stakeholders are more likely to take an 'I'll know it when I see it' approach. This is from page 83 under evolving and discovering requirements in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition.

question: You are gathering requirements for a new feature in a customer management system. The product owner has given a user interface feature as the next highest priority and you are collocated with the team receiving the benefits. What will you do next?

options.A: Show the customer team how the new user interface will look as they will be using it
options.B: Communicate the requirements to developers with a coherent logical flow of ideas
options.C: Elicit requirements into user stories using acceptance criteria that are clear, verifiable, and traceable
options.D: Show the mockup or prototype to the developers and ask them to develop based on that

explanation: Elicitation means to draw out. Well-documented requirements are clear, concise, verifiable, consistent, complete, and traceable. A prototype is useful but may need more information for developers to turn into a product. This is from page 83 under requirements elicitation in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition.

question: You are working in an agile team who are trying to break down the project requirements and the scope. They have created a scope statement and a work breakdown structure, but you are not sure how it will work now that they are using Sprints and user stories. What will you do next?

options.A: Assign activities to each of the scope items in the work breakdown structure and place them on a Gantt chart as a project schedule.
options.B: Create a product road map with the team using the lowest most detailed part of the work breakdown structure.
options.C: Group the work by large themes of customer value shown as epics, then break them down into smaller user stories to complete in each Sprint.
options.D: Ask the team to trace the requirements properly using a requirements traceability matrix.

explanation: One way to identify scope is by identifying themes of customer value grouped by a common factor such as functionality, data source, or security level. These are shown as epics, which are too big to complete in one Sprint, and broken down into smaller user stories that can be completed in one Sprint. This is covered on page 84 under scope decomposition in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your project team are trialing an agile way of working. The product owner has a clear vision for the product. There is confusion in the team as to how much work should be done on a user story before it is taken to the Sprint review and how they will know that a story is complete. What will you do next?

options.A: Refer to the user story acceptance criteria and your team's definition of done.
options.B: Review the user story with multiple users to ensure it is correct.
options.C: Ask the product owner to sign off on the completed user story.
options.D: Ensure quality testing on the user story is completed and passed.

explanation: Different ways to describe component completion include acceptance or completion criteria, technical performance measures, and the definition of done. Quality testing and product owner sign-off all feed into the acceptance criteria and definition of done. This is covered on page 85 under completion of deliverables in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You have been working closely with the quality manager of your project planning the budget for the project phase that is due to begin in the next two months. They mention the need to budget for appraisal costs to avoid quality issues in the future. What will you do next?

options.A: Ensure there is enough for training.
options.B: Ensure there is enough for a quality tester to verify the deliverables against the agreed specifications.
options.C: Ensure there is enough to create a quality assurance plan.
options.D: Ensure there is enough to allow for rework or rectification when defects are found.

explanation: The cost of quality includes prevention costs (training, quality planning), appraisal costs (verification, audits, testing), internal failure costs (waste, defects, rework), and external failure costs (customer complaints, repairs, warranty claims). Appraisal costs relate to verifying deliverables against agreed specifications. This is covered on page 89 under cost of quality in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your agile project has recently had multiple quality issues. Project features have been prioritized by the product owner and broken down into user stories. What will you do to help reduce the cost of quality issues the most?

options.A: Quality test each user story according to its acceptance criteria before release.
options.B: Advise the team that any quality defects will come out of their bonus.
options.C: Perform testing all in one go at the end to reduce the cost of testing resources.
options.D: Ensure a triad of developer, tester, and customer is involved during the requirements and design.

explanation: The cost of change is least during requirements and design, 20 times greater during the build, 50 times during test, and 150 times if a defect must be rectified in production. Involving a triad of developer, tester, and customer from the requirements and design phase ensures things are correct from the very beginning, which is the most cost-effective approach. If requirements and acceptance criteria are incorrect, extra quality testing alone will not help. This is covered on page 90 under the cost of change in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working in an agile team where executives in the organisation you are delivering to are asking for a large display of reports to be created every two weeks showing how the project is tracking and updating them on the cost and benefits. What will you do next?

options.A: Collect the planned value versus earned value to show the executives any variance in the plan.
options.B: Collocate the team with the executives so they can answer any questions as they arise.
options.C: Suggest a demonstration of the features developed during each Sprint instead, and a burndown chart of future product releases.
options.D: Create the desired reports for the executives to keep them happy.

explanation: The value of measurements is not in the collection and dissemination of data but in how that data is used to take appropriate action. Agile projects demonstrate value and features delivered, and use live information radiators instead of large reports to stakeholders. This is covered on page 94 under the measurement performance domain in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Multiple features have been released but they are not having the effect that the product owner believes they should. Your team are using some key performance indicators (KPIs) to track performance and you agree to move to more leading indicators. What could you use as examples of leading indicators on your project?

options.A: An increase in customer usage once the feature is released, such as sales and repeat purchasing.
options.B: Stakeholders who are not engaged and a lack of a risk management process.
options.C: Velocity of the work completed in each Sprint and cycle time of the user stories.
options.D: Variance in the schedule between planned and actual time taken.

explanation: There are two types of KPIs: leading indicators predict changes or trends in the project and include lack of a risk management process, stakeholders who are not engaged, and poorly defined project success criteria. Lagging indicators reflect what has already happened and include work completed, schedule variance, customer usage, and cycle time. This is covered on page 96 under leading indicators in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working in an agile project team that has increased in size and complexity since the project began. In reviewing the throughput of work with your team you notice fewer and fewer user stories being completed, and the road map of features scheduled to go live is being pushed out. What will you do next?

options.A: Place a limit on the work in progress and work with the team to reduce the cycle time.
options.B: Increase meetings with the team.
options.C: Ask the team to work overtime until the number of backlogs reduces.
options.D: Put a pull system in place where the team can pull work only when they are ready.

explanation: With more work, different tasks, and increased complexity, the team are likely context switching between too many different things. Kanban aims to limit the work in progress to avoid context switching and multitasking. Other approaches include reducing queue size and batch sizes and reviewing process efficiency. This is covered on page 99 under what to measure under delivery in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working on an agile project where the project customer wants a reliable way to measure the project performance and you first need a baseline. What measures will you show to your customer as part of your agile project?

options.A: Team velocity.
options.B: Start and finish dates.
options.C: The rate of features completed.
options.D: Schedule and cost variance.

explanation: An agile project tracks and demonstrates the actual features delivered. With a stable team, cost is most often fixed and the schedule is also fixed, while scope is variable depending on what the customer wants to prioritize. Team velocity and schedule or cost variance are internal team measures rather than customer-facing measures. This is covered on page 100 under baseline performance in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: The product owner has worked with a team of senior users to gather feature ideas. They now have a list of more than 50 ideas for your team to develop, which is far too many to complete in the time frame that you have. What will you do next?

options.A: Start work on the smallest features first.
options.B: Ask the product owner to return and ask for a shortened list.
options.C: Workshop the features with a wider audience to get a diverse view on their priority.
options.D: Ask the product owner to prioritize the features using cost to benefit and then deliver in their priority order until the time or money runs out.

explanation: Product owners prioritize features based on business value. Metrics that measure business value include cost to benefit ratio, planned to actual benefits delivery, return on investment, and net present value. The product owner is the single decision-maker for prioritization so the team can move much faster without needing a large group consensus. This is covered on page 102 under business value in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your agile project team has only recently formed. The product is still unclear and there have been disagreements and minor conflicts over the past two weeks as the team find their rhythm. You would like things to improve but you cannot quite pinpoint or address the problem. What will you do next?

options.A: Hold a workshop with your team and ask them why they are unhappy.
options.B: Have the team complete a net promoter score with comments and ask for improvement ideas at the next retrospective.
options.C: Raise the issue with their line managers and ensure that everyone is working on the issue.
options.D: Have the team pair up together so they can learn from each other.

explanation: Measuring stakeholders can be done with a net promoter score or a satisfaction score, especially with a comment or a mood chart. Improvement ideas are captured during retrospectives for most agile teams. Pairing is another agile practice but is not as relevant at this initial stage. This is covered on page 103 under measuring stakeholders in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: The project has faced serious impacts in getting materials with supply chains being disrupted and a global pandemic, and is currently 3 weeks behind schedule. You take this information to the next project steering committee meeting where they advise they need more information on how much the project will end up costing. What will you do next?

options.A: Use the current budget with no deviations until it runs out.
options.B: Use the project burndown chart to see the burn rate of accounting funds.
options.C: Use regression analysis to see the current rate of spending and forecast where the final cost will end up.
options.D: Use the project's estimate at completion (EAC) to show the steering committee the most likely final cost.

explanation: This question is about budget forecasts. Regression analysis may work but the best answer is estimate at completion (EAC), which is the budget at completion divided by CPI (cost performance index — earned value divided by actual cost). EAC shows the steering committee the most likely final cost. Page 104 under Forecasts in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You're working on an agile project where the project sponsor and executive managers require lengthy reports about the project work each week. This takes you and the project team a considerable amount of time to do and reduces time spent on creating the actual product. What will you do next?

options.A: Reuse most of the information each week so your time spent on reports is reduced.
options.B: Suggest the leaders come to the Sprint review and gather any information they need from the information radiator.
options.C: Ask your project sponsor to wait until a steering committee meeting for the information.
options.D: Share your working project schedule and budget with the executives instead of creating the reports.

explanation: Agile teams showcase actual work completed at the Sprint review. Information radiators, also known as big visible charts, are visible displays that allow anyone to pull information from them at any time — they are often low-tech and high-touch and contain information on work completed, remaining tasks, risks, kanban boards, and burndown charts. Page 108 under Information Radiators in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You're working on a project that has planned as much as they can up front and you would like them to move to a more agile way of working. Currently the work is hidden, the team catches up only once a week in a working group, and it's hard to see what each person is working on and to keep track of it. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask your team to report on their work in a written status update each day so you can keep track.
options.B: Have more working group meetings so you're always up to date on what the team is working on.
options.C: Ask the team to move their work to a central visible task board like a kanban board and then have a short meeting each day to discuss progress on their cards like a standup meeting.
options.D: Remove team meetings altogether and trust your team to get the work done.

explanation: This question is about standups and a kanban board. Task boards are part of the visual controls used where an increment of work moves from to-do to in-progress to done. The team meets for 15 minutes each day to discuss progress and raise any impediments (blockers) to their work. Page 109 Visual Controls / Task Boards in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You're working on a hybrid project where the majority of the work has been planned up front. The team manages their work via a kanban board and reports on progress and blockers each day. As the project evolves, stakeholders have added more and more scope to the product, with one executive raising an urgent feature that needs to be added. What will you do next?

options.A: Limit the number of cards in ready or to-do and ask the product owner to work with stakeholder requests, noting the need to swap the new item for an existing item.
options.B: Match the number of user stories to the team's velocity to ensure a sustainable pace.
options.C: Work with the stakeholder to raise a change request and note the outcome in a change log.
options.D: Review the impact of the scope change with the stakeholder using the work breakdown structure dictionary.

explanation: As a hybrid project using kanban and some agile ceremonies, the most appropriate way to manage the work and scope is by using a work-in-progress (WIP) limit on the board. The product owner has the final say on prioritizing features and scope, and works with stakeholders to ensure they understand that something might be deprioritized when adding a new feature. Page 110 under Kanban Board in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.
