question: You're working as a product owner in an agile team where the main measure of project success is the number of features delivered into production. Over the past 6 months, the team has delivered the highest number of features your organization has ever seen. However, customers are starting to complain about issues and bugs in the product. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask the team to work harder.
options.B: Adjust the product metrics to include customer satisfaction and quality measures.
options.C: Add a slack card of five points in the Sprint backlog to deal with the technical debt.
options.D: Remove customer comments and feedback completely until you've finished delivering all the features.

explanation: This is the Hawthorne Effect: what we measure influences the behavior of our team. In this case, measuring only the number of features caused the team to deliver features at the cost of quality and customer satisfaction. By adding measures for customer satisfaction and quality, that will balance out the team's behavior. Page 112 Measurement Pitfalls / Hawthorne Effect in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You've been working on a project for the past 8 months. Your project team has been approached by multiple stakeholders for reporting on various performance measures about the project and the product area. A large amount of their time is now spent creating these reports for others, and they've escalated this issue to you. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask the project stakeholders to stop interacting with your team.
options.B: Spend a portion of your project budget to automate the reports for your stakeholders.
options.C: Work with the team including the stakeholders to only measure things that will facilitate a decision, help avoid an issue, or increase project performance.
options.D: Invite your stakeholders to your Sprint reviews so they can see a working feature in practice.

explanation: The intent in measuring and displaying data is to learn and improve and to optimize project performance and efficiency. Only measure and report information that will allow the project team to learn, facilitate a decision, improve some aspect of performance, help avoid an issue, or help prevent performance deterioration in the future. Page 114 under Growing and Improving in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You're working on a project where there is a significant amount of uncertainty about the requirements. There is also a legislative change coming and no one knows what it will mean for your industry. As a project manager, how do you deal with this uncertainty?

options.A: Perform additional planning for your project to improve your view on scope management.
options.B: Hold additional retrospectives to capture challenges and improvements from your team.
options.C: Create a risk assessment of all possible project risks with their risk ratings and owners.
options.D: Gather information and prepare for multiple outcomes, and build team resilience through processes and training.

explanation: This question is about general uncertainty and risk. Uncertainty cannot be predicted precisely, and methods for responding to uncertainty include gathering information, preparing for multiple outcomes, using set-based design (e.g., prototypes), and building in resilience for the team. Page 118 under General Uncertainty in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You're working on a project with a vendor where there is significant ambiguity involved and multiple options that the team could go with. Many outcomes are possible that have varying degrees of impact. To improve the situation, what will you do next?

options.A: Use experiments and prototypes and progressive elaboration to work your way through it.
options.B: Ask your team for additional reporting around different options so you can make an informed decision.
options.C: Add all the different options into your project scope so all your bases are covered.
options.D: Work more closely with your senior users so they can inform you of the product requirements.

explanation: This question is on ambiguity. There are two types of ambiguity: conceptual (something is hard to understand) and situational (there are multiple options and outcomes). Use progressive elaboration, experiments, and prototypes to work through the ambiguity — deliver a prototype, get feedback, adjust, and iterate. Page 120 under Ambiguity in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You're working on a project to improve some software that has been in production for over 17 years and has been modified extensively over that time. It also integrates with many other systems within your organization. You cannot decide which features to deliver first. How will you deal with the complexity?

options.A: List out the possible features you can deliver and prioritize them by cost over benefit.
options.B: Simulate decoupling connecting parts to simplify the number of variables.
options.C: Reframe your view by brainstorming with a diverse set of stakeholders.
options.D: Provide an incentive for your project team for whoever can solve this complex problem.
options.E: Form a business case for a third-party system to replace the existing complex system to transfer risk of delivery.

explanation: This question is on complexity. Methods for dealing with complexity include systems-based decoupling and simulation, reframing by getting diversity and balanced views, process-based approaches (iterating forward, engaging stakeholders), and using error-proofing or fail-safe measures. Page 121 under Complexity in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You're working on a project that requires a very particular programming skill set that is not very common. It's also the hottest job market the industry has seen in two decades, so people are hard to come by and they leave often. The programming language is also evolving at a quick pace. What will you do next?

options.A: Develop the product from scratch in a more common programming language.
options.B: Ask your whole team to learn the programming skill set to cover the risk.
options.C: Use alternatives analysis to gather different options and ensure a sufficient cost reserve to help complete your project.
options.D: Raise a risk to your project completion with the threat of project team member skill sets.

explanation: This question is referring to volatility — an environment subject to rapid and unpredictable change, including ongoing fluctuations in available skill sets or materials. Alternatives analysis and cost reserves are ways to deal with volatility. Page 122 under Volatility in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your project team have found a significant data vulnerability in one of the features planned in your product backlog where customer data is exposed without password access. This feature is partially made by a third party vendor and the relationship is owned by a functional manager in your organization. What will you do next?

options.A: Avoid the risk and wait for the third party vendor to fix the vulnerability.
options.B: Transfer the risk by asking a different vendor to create a feature with no vulnerability in their design.
options.C: Escalate the risk to the manager with the appropriate authority to act on the response.
options.D: Accept the risk and move on to other features so the work continues to get done.

explanation: With project risks and responses to threats there are five main types: avoiding, escalating, transferring, mitigating, and accepting. Escalation is appropriate when the project team agrees that the threat is outside the scope of the project or exceeds the project manager's authority. This is page 123 in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition under overall project risks and threats.

question: You brainstorm risk responses with your project team and project sponsor and decide you cannot transfer a certain high impact risk. You also can't avoid the risk and there is no one in the organization to escalate it to. Your project sponsor agrees that you should actively accept the risk. What will you do next?

options.A: Develop a contingency plan that would be triggered if the event occurred.
options.B: Shift the ownership to a third party to manage the risk and bear the impact if it does occur.
options.C: Act to eliminate the threat or protect the project from its impact only.
options.D: Take action to reduce the probability of its occurrence and the impact of the threat.

explanation: This question describes the various risk responses to threats. Actively accepting a risk can include developing a contingency plan which would be triggered if the event occurred. Passive acceptance means just accepting it and doing nothing. Acting to eliminate the threat is avoiding it, and taking action to reduce the probability of occurrence or impact is mitigating. This is page 123 under risk threats in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You're working on a project to build a new customer database system for an engineering team. In working with the requirements you realize that this system can also work with other teams in your organization and the effort to add those teams is actually quite small. What will you do next?

options.A: Escalate the opportunity to work with the other teams and their managers.
options.B: Exploit the opportunity to actively make sure that it happens.
options.C: Enhance the opportunity by increasing the probability of its occurrence.
options.D: Accept the opportunity and merely acknowledge that it exists.

explanation: When dealing with opportunities in your project there are five main responses: exploit is acting to ensure an opportunity occurs; escalating is when it's outside the authority of the project manager; sharing allocates ownership to a third party best able to capture its benefit; enhancing is increasing the probability of its occurrence; and accepting merely acknowledges that it is existing without doing anything extra. This is page 125 under risks and opportunities in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You're working as a product owner in an agile team and during a recent risk review the team have uncovered various risks that will impact your project. You brainstorm risk responses with your team. The project customer would like them to be mitigated as soon as possible. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask your team to work on the risk responses and get them done as soon as possible.
options.B: Ignore the risks initially as some might dissipate over time and only the important ones will be left.
options.C: Assign the risk responses to all stakeholders around your project team to share the load.
options.D: Prioritize the risks by comparing the expected monetary value of the risk to the anticipated return on investment.

explanation: Prioritizing risk responses in a risk-adjusted backlog is just as important as prioritizing features to avoid negative risk impacts and technical damage. Comparing the expected monetary value of a risk to the anticipated return on investment of a deliverable allows product owners to incorporate risk responses into the planned work in a normal way. This information is from the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You're planning a project where supply chains have been impacted and the requirements are complex and intertwined with other areas of the business. Your project sponsor asks you to manage these risks before the project moves to execution. What will you do next?

options.A: Move to project execution so you can work through risks as they happen.
options.B: Move to an agile way of work so you can deliver faster and also deal with the complexity.
options.C: Note the risks with appropriate controls and add a contingency reserve in the project budget.
options.D: Assign a management reserve to deal with the risks before moving to project execution.

explanation: Reserves are an amount of time or budget set aside to account for handling risks. Contingency reserve is for identified risks should they occur. Management reserve is for unknown events such as unplanned in-scope work. This is page 127 management and contingency reserve in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You're working on an agile project and delivering a usable feature every few weeks. The project sponsor is from a waterfall background and is concerned that there is not enough focus on risk. They would like to hold a regular risk review with a wide group of stakeholders. What will you suggest instead?

options.A: There is no need for a separate meeting as risk is everyone's responsibility in an agile team.
options.B: Use daily standups to report blockers early and fortnightly demonstrations of the product to catch any stakeholder dissatisfaction.
options.C: Use the kanban board to see the work and how it flows.
options.D: Hold the risk meeting but only with the immediate project team to avoid unnecessary participants.

explanation: In agile teams the risk review can take the form of the daily standup reporting blockers that could become threats if they continue to delay progress. Frequent demonstrations of the product can also surface threats and opportunities as can retrospectives. While quality is everyone's responsibility and the kanban board does visualize the work, B is the best answer. This is page 127 under risk review in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your project management office has no prescribed way of work so they're working with a vendor who supplies an extreme programming framework at a significant cost. Projects are still running behind schedule, over budget, and with low customer satisfaction. What will you do next?

options.A: Tailor the approach to suit the organization operating environment and project needs.
options.B: Use a waterfall approach instead to ensure the proper planning.
options.C: Switch to Scrum instead as it's a more popular framework.
options.D: Tighten the controls around the project environment with a more directive PMO approach.

explanation: Projects operate in complex environments that need to balance competing demands. Tailoring is performed to better suit the organization operating environment and project needs. This is page 132 under tailoring overview in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your project management office is in the middle of a company-wide ways of working transformation. They have taken a popular project framework and are tailoring it to the organization with branded templates and processes that meet their existing governance requirements. You've been asked to help finalize the process. What will you do next?

options.A: Gather feedback from the functional business areas to ensure the process meets their needs.
options.B: Roll out the new way of working to the PMO as a pilot then to the rest of the organization.
options.C: Enable a process for tailoring based on size and complexity and implement ongoing improvement.
options.D: Form a working group comprised of executives and project professionals.

explanation: This question is referring to the tailoring process which is: select the initial development approach, tailor for the organization, tailor for the project, and implement ongoing improvement. This is page 137 the tailoring process in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You're a manager of a PMO in a large organization which has recently undertaken a company-wide way of working transformation. You need to select an initial development approach for your project which you will then tailor further to suit them. What will you do next?

options.A: Use a framework that best engages your executive team through easy to use graphics and presentations.
options.B: Select the most popular current framework to keep up with what other organizations are doing.
options.C: Select multiple development approaches as a baseline for your organization.
options.D: Use a suitability filter to assess the most appropriate development approach.

explanation: A suitability filter helps project teams consider whether a project has characteristics that lend themselves towards a predictive, hybrid, or adaptive approach, then select one of them as a base and enable the ability to tailor to your project or organization's circumstances. This is page 138 select initial development approach in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your organization is starting to move to an agile way of work and they're setting up a value delivery office (VDO) to complement the existing PMO. The executives in the area have begun working through what the function looks like and the roles and responsibilities that might exist. They approach you for help. What will you tell them?

options.A: A VDO is responsible for the value delivered and owns the programs within the organization.
options.B: The VDO focuses on coaching project teams, building adaptive skills, growing capabilities, and mentoring sponsors and product owners to be more effective.
options.C: The VDO is responsible for agile resources such as Scrum Masters and product owners to be allocated effectively to project teams.
options.D: The VDO creates the organizational framework to be used on each project throughout the company.

explanation: A VDO serves an enabling role rather than a management or oversight function. It focuses on coaching project teams, building adaptive skills and capabilities throughout the organization, and mentoring sponsors and product owners. This is page 141 tailor for the organization the value delivery office in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You've been planning a project for the last 2 months, gathering requirements in scope and assigning tasks to a schedule. As you get closer to the execution phase of your project, you decide what delivery approach you will use. The requirements are likely to change, require a medium amount of compliance, but can be released in one go. What will you do next?

options.A: Use a predictive delivery approach, planning everything up front and delivering in one go
options.B: Use an incremental delivery approach, releasing increments of value over time
options.C: Use an agile or adaptive delivery approach, gathering feedback and releasing incrementally
options.D: Use an iterative delivery approach, gathering feedback and improving over time while delivering in one release

explanation: Gathering feedback and improving over time while delivering in one release is an iterative delivery approach. Incremental releases increments of value like features, predictive plans everything up front and delivers in one go, and agile gathers feedback and releases incrementally. Page 141 under tailor for your project product and deliverable in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You have been asked to make a process improvement in the accounting area for your organization. You have been assigned a small team of nine people, each with all the skills you need to complete the project. The team is collocated and has unlimited access to the project customer. How will you tailor your delivery approach?

options.A: Use a predictive delivery approach as lots of planning will be needed
options.B: Use an agile or adaptive delivery approach as the team is small, collocated, cross-functional and has close access to the customer
options.C: Use an incremental delivery approach to deliver features over time
options.D: Use a hybrid delivery approach combining waterfall and agile

explanation: An agile or adaptive delivery approach works best when the team is small (fewer than 9 to 12 people), has close access to the customer, is cross-functional so everyone has the skills required, and is collocated in the same space. Predictive is best when lots of planning is needed, hybrid is best when two different approaches are needed, and incremental delivers features over time. Page 141 under tailor for the project team in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You have been working as a project manager in an organization for some time where an agile-centric delivery approach and way of work has been selected and tailored for the organization. Over the past few months you notice several things are not working and could be improved. As you begin new projects, what will you do next?

options.A: Review the product backlog to see the flow of features going through the teams
options.B: Look into issues, threats and quality assurance statistics such as defects
options.C: Suggest a retrospective with your PMO leaders to determine ongoing improvement opportunities, discussing what worked well and what did not
options.D: Review a control chart and value stream map to see non-value-added activities and remove waste in the process

explanation: In an agile environment, holding a retrospective to identify what is working well and what is challenging or could be improved is the best way to find ongoing improvements, going directly to the source and asking for feedback and improving as a team. Page 151 tailoring diagnostics for agile teams in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You have been working on a project to deliver a new customer data system and have noticed that the work is starting to slow down — fewer user stories are appearing in your Sprint backlog as they are not ready. You hold a retrospective with your team and discover there are long delays waiting for requirements and acceptance criteria approvals. What will you do next?

options.A: Streamline approval decisions through fewer people such as a product owner authorized to make decisions up to certain value thresholds
options.B: Ask your team to place the user stories in the Sprint backlog, reducing the need for customer approval
options.C: Note the long delays as a risk to your project with a high likelihood as it is already happening
options.D: Escalate the issue to the functional manager to ensure approvals are happening quickly

explanation: Where there are long delays or waiting for approvals, the tailoring suggestion is to streamline decisions through fewer people authorized to make decisions up to certain value thresholds. Page 151 common situations and tailoring suggestions in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You have been assigned to take over an existing project as the previous project manager leaves for another opportunity. In working with your team you notice there is a large amount of work currently in progress all at once, with team members working on multiple items at a time and constantly context switching between tasks. What will you do next?

options.A: Add more people to the project
options.B: Use value stream mapping and Kanban boards to visualize the work and brainstorm solutions with your team
options.C: Ask your team to work overtime to cover the extra work and bring it all back in line
options.D: Review the scope of the project and reduce items with low impact to customer satisfaction

explanation: Where there is too much work in progress or high rates of scrap, it is recommended to use techniques like value stream mapping and Kanban boards to visualize the work, identify the issues, and propose solutions. Adding more people can also complicate communication and processes. Page 151 in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition under common situations and tailoring suggestions.

question: The product owner has prioritized the product backlog of features and the team creates user stories with estimates and places enough of them to fill a 2-week Sprint. You notice that the pieces being completed are being rejected by the customer for defects and missed requirements. What will you do next?

options.A: Create the project scope from scratch with the project team and customers
options.B: Have an iteration zero planning Sprint with your project team to set it up for success
options.C: Add more feedback loops such as shoulder checks, peer review, unit testing and system testing
options.D: Have less frequent customer demonstrations so they only see the product when it is correct

explanation: When a product is facing poor quality deliverables, the tailoring suggestion is to add more feedback loops or feedback verification loops and quality assurance steps, including demonstrations and reviews, peer review, shoulder checks, pair programming, quality audits, unit testing, system testing, user acceptance testing, regression testing and automated testing. Page 151 under common situations and tailoring suggestions in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your project team has started to find their rhythm, yet you notice that they seem disengaged in their work and are dragging their feet on normal tasks. You check your project budget and note that the team are all paid fairly for the market and within the same range for the role. What will you do next?

options.A: Look for ways to reward the team members with bonuses above their normal pay rate
options.B: Move team members around to different roles frequently so they experience job variety
options.C: Give the team a paid day off
options.D: Establish ways for the team to grow and advance

explanation: Frederick Herzberg's study of motivational factors found there are hygiene factors (company policies, salary, physical environment) and motivational factors. Once hygiene factors are sufficient, motivational factors such as achievement, growth and advancement provide satisfaction. Paying bonuses or a paid day off might seem like a good idea but will fade and not address the root cause of the engagement. Page 158 in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition, hygiene and motivational factors.

question: You are working as a project manager on a regulatory change for your organization. You notice that the team are not communicating as well as they could be, and you put together a plan to improve team communication by increasing its effectiveness and richness. What will you do?

options.A: Include methods that will handle multiple information cues such as body language, voice, and words, facilitate rapid feedback, and utilize natural language
options.B: Include methods to escalate issues quickly with executives being open for anything the team needs
options.C: Include ways to get sign-off in writing so it can be recorded and reviewed later when needed
options.D: Include communication that ensures everyone gets a voice and input into decisions

explanation: Alistair Cockburn developed a model that describes communication channels along the axes of effectiveness and richness. Media richness includes the ability to handle multiple information cues, rapid feedback, establish a personal focus, and utilize natural language — for example face-to-face communication with collocated teams. Page 157 under effectiveness of communication channels in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your deliverables have been signed off by the product owner and they are ready to be released to the customer, which is the financial analytics team of your organization. The new system will be quite a change from their normal day-to-day work. The team already have the desire to use the new system. What will you do next?

options.A: Create a vision and explain the why so the team understands and agrees with the change
options.B: Use training and education to show them the new processes and systems
options.C: Give them unlimited hands-on practice so they can get used to the new system
options.D: Include rewards, recognition, feedback and measurement once you have released it to them

explanation: The ADKAR model for change management covers Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability and Reinforcement. Once people have the desire for change, the next step is knowledge, which includes training and education for the new processes and systems. Page 161 under the ADKAR model in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are leading a large organizational change that affects more than a thousand people across multiple teams. You have engaged with the senior managers of that area, created a brief vision statement that summarizes the change, created a strategy to realize the vision, and communicated the vision throughout the change process. What will you do next?

options.A: Release multiple usable features to the affected teams so they can get used to it gradually
options.B: Anchor the changes into corporate culture by telling success stories and recognizing people
options.C: Address any obstacles to the change including outdated processes and people resistance
options.D: Set goals for continued improvement on the change throughout the organization

explanation: John Kotter's eight-step process for leading change is: create urgency, form a powerful coalition, create a vision, communicate the vision, remove obstacles, create short-term wins, build on the change, and anchor the changes in corporate culture. After communicating the vision, the next step is removing obstacles. Page 162 under the eight-step process for leading change in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are assessing the risk of future releases with your team. You have a clear set of requirements from your customer, however there are a range of possible correct solutions. There are a range of known unknowns. How are you going to approach this situation?

options.A: Use current best practices to make a decision.
options.B: Assess the facts, analyze the situation, and apply known good practices.
options.C: Use emergent practices that will allow you to probe the environment, sense the situation, and respond with action.
options.D: Sense where there is some stability and take steps to try and stabilize the situation.

explanation: The Cynefin framework describes complicated relationships when there is a set of known unknowns or a range of correct answers. In complicated situations it is best to assess the facts, analyze the situation, and apply good practices. Complex situations (unknown unknowns) call for probing the environment, and chaotic situations require stabilization first.

question: You've been asked to initiate a project to deliver a new system. The requirements are not clear and it will take some time to uncover them. The system has been used by some team members but not all, and it has a medium level of uncertainty around it. How are you going to approach this project?

options.A: Release small usable features and improve your approach based on feedback.
options.B: Do additional planning up front to ensure every component is thought of.
options.C: Split the team into two and work on different parts of the system to simplify it.
options.D: Form a business case for starting from scratch with new easier-to-use technology.

explanation: The Stacey Matrix looks at two dimensions to determine complexity: uncertainty in the requirements and uncertainty in the technology. Agile methodologies work best in complicated and complex environments. Releasing small usable features allows the team to work through uncertainty iteratively. Page 165 under Stacey Matrix in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: Your project team came together from different parts of the organization and were introduced formally to each other. You notice that there is some conflict in the team — some people don't respect others' decisions and are not working together well, and there are some personality clashes within the team. What are you going to do next?

options.A: Help team members get to know each other and learn their names.
options.B: Help the team understand their roles and responsibilities and form a regular rhythm of meetings, collaboration, and work.
options.C: Help the team jockey for position within the team and foster healthy conflict.
options.D: Help the team complete project work and disperse onto other things.

explanation: This scenario describes the five stages of team development from the Tuckman Ladder. When there is conflict and difficulty working together the team is in the storming stage. We should help them understand their roles and responsibilities and find a regular rhythm so they can move to the norming stage. Page 166 under Tuckman Ladder in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You have formed a new project team and have worked through the purpose and mission of the project with them. You expanded on who is on the project team and the skills and abilities each person brings. You would like to foster a high-performing culture and ensure the team is able to deliver as soon as possible. What are you going to do next?

options.A: Work through changes on the project team and the project such as deliverables and stakeholders.
options.B: Decompose the high-level plan into greater detail including a detailed schedule or backlog.
options.C: Define a plan to achieve the project goals including milestone schedules, release plans, and high-level budgets.
options.D: Clarify the goals of the team and what you are here to deliver.

explanation: This question refers to the Drexler-Sibbet Team Performance Model. The steps include orientation, trust building, goal clarification, commitment, implementation, high performance, and renewal. After trust building comes goal clarification, so clarifying the goals and what the team is here to deliver is the next step. Page 167 under Drexler-Sibbet Team Performance Model in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: Your team members have raised an issue as they believe the current project scope will not reach the intended project goal of increasing customer retention. You take their concerns to the project sponsor who controls the funding and resources. The sponsor dismisses them and directs you to proceed with the scope currently set. What will you do next?

options.A: Problem solve with the sponsor and find a resolution.
options.B: Compromise with the sponsor so each party gets a little bit of what they want.
options.C: Accommodate the sponsor to maintain harmony and goodwill.
options.D: Force the issue so that the sponsor accepts the change.

explanation: Ken Thomas and Ralph Kilmann describe six ways to address conflict depending on the relative power between individuals: confronting/problem solving, collaborating, compromising, accommodating, forcing, and withdrawing. Since the sponsor has significant power and there is a desire to maintain a good relationship, adopting an accommodating posture is appropriate in this scenario. Page 168-169 under the conflict model in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: The functional manager raises a concern that they have not been engaged correctly and the program is missing some key requirements as a result. You believe you've captured all the items required. Your program manager asks you to handle the situation and approach it from a win-win perspective. What will you do next?

options.A: Escalate the decision to the steering committee to ensure your point of view is heard.
options.B: Demonstrate integrity, find mutual trust, and look at the situation from their point of view.
options.C: Choose to give some leeway in your approach so the functional manager can add their requirements.
options.D: Get your requirements baselined first and then compete with the manager to ensure a win.

explanation: Steven Covey's approach from The Seven Habits of Highly Effective People describes the win-win outcome. A win-win approach is found when there is mature character, trust, and the ability to approach it from the other's point of view. Page 169 under negotiation in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You have been planning a project to deliver a process change for the past four weeks. The requirements and scope have been signed off by the business owner and the team are moving into delivery and execution. The work begins in line with the project plan and scope. What will you do next?

options.A: Monitor and control the project work.
options.B: Execute the project work.
options.C: Plan the work.
options.D: Formally sign off the project.

explanation: The project management process groups are initiating, planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, and closing. As project work is executed it then needs to be monitored and controlled to ensure it stays on track and meets its goals. Page 170 under process groups in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You are working as a product owner. While your sales and revenue have been increasing over the past eight months, the overall profit has decreased by 17%. Your executive manager asks you to address the situation. What are you going to do next?

options.A: Stop the current work and set a planning session with your team to address the situation.
options.B: Ask your team to work overtime on the current features in your backlog to get the work done.
options.C: Perform benchmarking with a similar yet non-competitive company and add any process improvements to your product backlog.
options.D: Look for opportunities to partner with other products in your organization and increase your sales further.

explanation: As a product owner your responsibility is to increase users, sales, revenue, and profit for your product. If profit is decreasing while sales revenue is increasing, the product is costing more to produce or sell. Performing benchmarking to find gaps in your process and then executing improvements will help address the root cause. Page 175 under data gathering and analysis: benchmarking in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You have been given authority to manage the upcoming features your team will release. You do not have a high enough level of expertise in the product, so you gather senior users from the product's area to create a features list. Each stakeholder wants their feature to be delivered first. How will you prioritize them and move forward?

options.A: Go with the features wanted by the top-level executives first to avoid issues later on.
options.B: Perform MoSCoW analysis with the stakeholders.
options.C: Perform a root cause analysis and go with the features that address the root cause.
options.D: Use a cost-benefit analysis and compare the time needed to recover the project cost.

explanation: Cost-to-benefit and payback period (time to recover an investment) are the best accepted ways to prioritize business value. Root cause analysis is better for specific problems, and performing MoSCoW with stakeholders may result in each of them wanting their feature to be 'must have'. Page 175 under business justification analysis methods in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You're creating a business case and a project charter with a small initial project team. You have a high-level feature decomposed into smaller release milestones and you'd like to note a high-level schedule using wideband Delphi or a high-level version of planning poker. What are you going to do next?

options.A: Hold a round of cards with points on them where each team member places their cards in the center for the total estimate.
options.B: Ask subject matter experts to complete multiple rounds of estimates individually, compare and explain the highest and lowest estimates for each round.
options.C: Show the wideband high-level range from the lowest to highest for each feature.
options.D: Find the business functionality for each feature and estimate the effort based on that.

explanation: The wideband Delphi technique asks subject matter experts to complete multiple rounds of estimates individually, then compare and explain the highest and lowest estimates for each round until estimates converge. Planning poker is a variation of wideband Delphi. Page 178 under estimating: wideband Delphi in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You have been asked to help facilitate a senior planning meeting in your organization. High-level executives have made a presentation on the strengths and opportunities of the organization, painting a rosy picture. Revenue has fallen recently and the chief executive asks you to level out their analysis with a more complete view. What will you do next?

options.A: Add your organization's competitors and compare their products
options.B: Showcase the pipeline of upcoming work to be delivered in the next quarter
options.C: Add the background and abilities of the executive team that will allow the organization to deliver
options.D: Brainstorm and capture the weaknesses of the organization and threats to the organization

explanation: This question refers to SWOT analysis — strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats. Once strengths and opportunities have been captured, a complete picture also involves the weaknesses and threats. Page 175 under SWOT analysis in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are adding an essential feature to your product road map. Your team believes they can complete the feature in 6 weeks. However, the feature is quite large and a bit more complicated than normal. One of your lead developers says they worked on a similar feature in another company and it took around 4 months to complete. What will you do next?

options.A: Use relative estimating
options.B: Use analogous estimating
options.C: Use parametric estimating
options.D: Use function point estimating to show the amount of business functionality in the feature

explanation: When the effort on a feature or deliverable needs to be estimated and there are people who have experience delivering a similar item, you can use analogous estimating — it is an analogy to something similar — to estimate the effort quickly and with a medium level of accuracy. Page 178 under analogous estimating in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You have a prioritized backlog of features. You have decomposed the next feature to be delivered into user stories to complete a Sprint of two weeks. The team would like to estimate the effort involved on those user stories but are unsure on how to proceed. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask each team member to add their own estimates as they complete the acceptance criteria
options.B: Estimate the effort for the overall feature and reduce the need for individual story estimates
options.C: Help the team perform relative estimating
options.D: Base the estimates on cost per hour and add them after the work is done to ensure accuracy

explanation: Relative estimating is the most common method for user stories, where they are sized in comparison to the smallest piece, often in story points and often on a Fibonacci scale. Page 178 under relative estimating in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are halfway through your current Sprint of 2 weeks. The work is progressing well, however you notice that there is not enough work ready and elaborated for when the next Sprint begins. What will you do next?

options.A: Hold a backlog refinement meeting and work with the team to identify the work that could be accomplished
options.B: Raise the issue as a blocker in the daily standup and ask your team to swarm around the problem
options.C: Raise the issue in the next retrospective and take an action for the team
options.D: Place the cards on the Kanban board so the team can see the work and get started

explanation: At a backlog refinement meeting, the backlog is progressively elaborated and reprioritized to identify the work that can be accomplished in an upcoming iteration. Page 179, meetings and events, backlog refinement in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You have completed a make or buy analysis in your project and found that part of your deliverable is best created by an external vendor. You work with your project team to create the procurement strategy, source selection criteria, and statement of work. What will you do next?

options.A: Gather the seller proposals including technical details and price
options.B: Meet with prospective sellers to ensure all vendors have a common understanding of the procurement
options.C: Perform source selection analysis on what you want from prospective vendors
options.D: Create the procurement agreement for the third party vendor to provide their specified services

explanation: A bidder conference is a meeting with prospective sellers prior to the preparation of a bid or proposal to ensure all prospective bidders have a clear and common understanding of the procurement. This meeting may also be known as contractor conferences, vendor conferences, or pre-bid conferences. Page 179 under bidder conference in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: One of the stakeholders in your project has raised a change request after some of the deliverables contained defects and a missed requirement. Your project has a formal change control process as opposed to a more agile prioritized product backlog. What will you do next?

options.A: Gather feedback from other stakeholders to see if they also need the change
options.B: Note the change in the change log and place the requested item in the next Sprint
options.C: Take it to the Change Control Board, then communicate the decision to approve or reject the change
options.D: Raise the request as an issue that will affect your project schedule and budget

explanation: Where formal change control processes are in place, a Change Control Board includes the people who are accountable for reviewing, approving, or rejecting changes to the project. The decisions made at this meeting are recorded and communicated to the appropriate stakeholders. Page 179 under Change Control Board in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your agile project has recently gained some new stakeholders after a department restructure. They complain to you that they are not seeing enough traction of work delivered through the project and they are worried that it will not meet their requirements. What will you do next?

options.A: Invite them to the daily standup so they can see the work that happens on a daily basis
options.B: Invite them to iteration planning so they can choose what the team works on
options.C: Invite them to the retrospective so they can raise their issue there and come up with a solution
options.D: Invite them to the iteration review so they can see the usable increment that the team delivers

explanation: The iteration or Sprint review is held at the end of an iteration, usually of 2 weeks, to demonstrate the work that was accomplished during the iteration to the project customers and stakeholders. Page 179 under iteration review in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You have recently taken over a project. Your project team approached you to say that there is a regulatory approval that has not been granted yet and it might block development of your project in the next 5 months. You check your project documentation but cannot see any mention of this. What will you do next?

options.A: Hold a risk review meeting, capture it as a risk, and ensure an adequate risk response
options.B: Hold a retrospective with your team to capture what challenges the team are facing
options.C: Adjust your product road map to reflect the changes to the time frame of that deliverable
options.D: Escalate the regulatory approval to the project steering committee

explanation: A risk review is a meeting to analyze the status of existing risks and to identify new risks, including changes to probability, impact, or urgency, and determining if responses are adequate. Page 180 under risk review in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are an agile project manager holding daily standups with your team. You notice people accusing other team members of not doing their job and there are some tasks that are not mentioned anywhere and no one is responsible for. The team velocity has slowed in the last three Sprints. What will you do next?

options.A: Refer to the team charter and the team roles and agreed ways of managing conflict
options.B: Raise it as an issue in the next retrospective and come to a solution with the team
options.C: Assign team roles to those you think are best suited
options.D: Work with your team to size user stories correctly so velocity improves

explanation: A retrospective is a regularly occurring workshop similar to a lessons learned meeting where the team explore their work in order to improve their process and the product. If information is not captured anywhere, the agile approach is to problem solve with the team in a retrospective. Page 180 under retrospective in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are managing a large construction project. Due to some delays, the project schedule has been pushed back by one week. As a result, one of the project risks has increased slightly but is still within the acceptable limits. What will you do next?

options.A: Hold a risk review and update the risk response and owners
options.B: Raise a change request for the change to the project schedule
options.C: Capture the change in your project reporting and take it to the next status meeting
options.D: Update the Sprint backlog to reflect the delayed work

explanation: A status meeting is a regularly scheduled event to exchange and analyze information about the current progress of the project and its performance. Since the risk is still within acceptable limits, communicating the schedule change to stakeholders via the status meeting is the appropriate step. Page 180 under status meeting in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are an agile project manager and your team have delivered three features to improve a customer app over the last 3 months. Your product owner needs to know if the features are having the desired effect. It is a large organization and there are multiple measures available. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask them to gather the team velocity and throughput over the past 3 months.
options.B: Ask them to gather the cost benefit ratio over the past 6 months.
options.C: Ask them to gather the NPS (net promoter score) over the past 6 months.
options.D: Ask them to gather the average app availability and uptime.

explanation: A product owner prioritizes features to improve usability and profitability of a product. The net promoter score measures the willingness of customers to recommend a product or service to others and is used to gauge overall customer satisfaction and loyalty to the brand or product. Page 181, net promoter score, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are a project manager in an agile team developing new features for an app. An executive stakeholder with high influence in the organization approaches you and asks you to add a feature that they like. They asked their friends and their friends think it is a good idea as well. What will you do next?

options.A: Place the feature in the product backlog and prioritize it using cost versus benefit.
options.B: Raise a change request to add the new feature into the scope baseline.
options.C: Gather the requirements for the new feature and ask your team to elaborate on it.
options.D: Analyze the impact to your project schedule and cost and add the feature to your project scope.

explanation: In agile we have a product backlog that we prioritize. Prioritization schemas are methods used to prioritize portfolio, program, or project components such as features, requirements, or even risks. Examples include multicriteria decision matrices, MoSCoW (must have, should have, could have, won't have), cost to benefit, and more. Page 181, prioritization schema, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your project team are gathering solution ideas for a particular customer requirement which is prioritized to be worked on next. The item is complicated and they have been trying to find a way forward for the past 3 weeks. There is not enough work for the upcoming Sprint. What will you do next?

options.A: Start work on the item in the next Sprint anyway and elaborate as the Sprint begins.
options.B: Arrange the right people to elaborate the issue with a spike, close in person, place and time, and time-box it to two days.
options.C: Deprioritize the item until the team can figure out the issue.
options.D: Adjust the project schedule to reflect the extra time taken on elaboration.

explanation: A spike is a short dedicated user story to research or analyze something. A time box is a short fixed period of time in which work is to be completed, often a few days to a few weeks. Page 181, time box, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your project team have planned the requirements, scope, schedule, and cost for constructing a new building in your organization. An executive from another area approaches you as your project is funded and about to begin execution, and asks if you can help them deliver a website as part of your work. What will you do next?

options.A: Raise the request as an issue in your project's issue log.
options.B: Add the website to your project scope and adjust your project schedule and cost.
options.C: Raise a change request for the added scope and review the impact to your project schedule and cost.
options.D: Offer to help them create a business case for the new idea and get funding for a new project.

explanation: The scope and team required to build a website is very different from a physical building; a new project with its own funding is the best option. A business case is a value proposition for a proposed project that might include financial and non-financial benefits; it typically precedes a project charter and a formal project approval and kickoff. Page 184, business case, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working on an agile project and you are planning your product backlog for the next 3 to 6 months. You have some high-level features or feature ideas from various parts of your organization, from frontline staff to executives to direct customer research as well. What will you do next?

options.A: Break down each feature into epics and use the stories, then size the work with your team.
options.B: Add all of the items into your product backlog and work on the ones from the managers first.
options.C: Work with the requesters and your technical experts to create a business model canvas for each feature.
options.D: Perform precedence diagramming on each feature to determine their dependencies.

explanation: The business model canvas is a one-page visual summary that describes the value proposition, the infrastructure, customers, and finances. They are often used in lean startup situations and can feed into prioritization of the product backlog. Page 184, business model canvas, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working in a new agile team where the work has started to slow and there is uncertainty and low team engagement. You set time to create a team charter with your team, agree on roles and responsibilities, and team cadence. What will you do next?

options.A: Set a team goal for them to be more engaged in the work over the next 3 months.
options.B: Create a product vision statement showing the value you are bringing and the why behind the work.
options.C: Ask the team to contribute more to all pieces of work during each team meeting.
options.D: Raise it as a people issue for your project and manage it with the project steering committee.

explanation: The project vision statement is a concise high-level description of the project that states the purpose and inspires the team to contribute to the project. Page 184, project vision statement, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are integrating a third-party system as part of your project. The developers in your project team have not worked on this system before but they believe the language and layout is the same and they should be able to implement it. Adding a bit of time for the uncertainty, they give a high-level estimate of 5 weeks. What will you do next?

options.A: Note the estimate of 5 weeks and update the assumption log with the technical assumption that the languages and layout is the same.
options.B: Don't add the estimate; break down the integration into user stories and use a bottom-up approach.
options.C: Check the estimate using parametric estimating to increase its accuracy.
options.D: Gather your whole team and customers together and estimate using wideband Delphi or planning poker.

explanation: An assumption is a factor that is considered to be true, real, or certain without proof or demonstration. A constraint is a factor that limits the options for managing a project. An assumption log records all assumptions and constraints throughout the project. Page 185, assumption log, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your agile project has delivered three features so far and is halfway through delivering the latest improvement. During a brainstorming session, one of your customers comes up with a new feature idea. You check and the benefit is high and the complexity is also low. What will you do next?

options.A: Add money to your budget to accommodate the additional feature as soon as possible.
options.B: Elaborate on the feature, break it down into user stories, and estimate them.
options.C: Place the item in the backlog and reprioritize it to be next.
options.D: Create a business case for the new feature outlining the benefit and costs involved.

explanation: A backlog is an ordered list of work to be done. Projects may have a product backlog, a requirement backlog, an impediment backlog, and many other things. Items in a backlog are prioritized and prioritized work is scheduled for upcoming iterations or sprints. Page 185, logs and registers — backlog, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: One of the senior users testing your product has found that it was missing a key requirement that he had assumed would be there. It was not noted in the project scope, which has been baselined already. You check the impact to cost and schedule, raise a change request on his behalf, and take it to the Change Control Board where it is approved. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask your team to elaborate and turn the requirement into user stories.
options.B: Place the new requirement into the Sprint backlog for the next Sprint.
options.C: Add the missed requirement to the issue log and problem-solve with your team to ensure it does not happen again in the future.
options.D: Note the outcome in the change log and communicate it to your stakeholders.

explanation: A change log is a comprehensive list of changes submitted during the project and their current status. A change can be a modification to any formally controlled deliverable, baseline document, project management plan, or project document. Once approved by the Change Control Board, the first best next step is to note the outcome in the change log and communicate it to affected stakeholders. Page 185, logs and registers — change log, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working on a hybrid project to replace an aging customer system that has just had a feature upgrade release. The senior developer raises a concern during the daily standup that the system doesn't seem to be able to connect to some parts of the organization after the upgrade. What will you do next?

options.A: Replan the project to ensure the correct scope is worked on that will connect to the organization.
options.B: Raise it in the issue log, assign it to a responsible party, and work with them to find a resolution.
options.C: Raise the concern as a risk and brainstorm risk responses.
options.D: Escalate the issue to the technical managers so they can resolve it.

explanation: An issue is a current condition or situation that may have an impact on the project objectives. An issue log is used to record and monitor information on active issues; issues are assigned to a responsible party for follow-up and resolution. Since this has already happened it is an issue, not a risk. Page 185, logs and registers — issue log, in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are working on an agile project where your project team have raised multiple risks to project delivery. Some of these risks may impact your cost and schedule significantly. Your program manager asks you to manage them using a risk adjusted backlog. What will you do next?

options.A: Adjust your risk register to include the work not completed if the risks are triggered.
options.B: Adjust your backlog velocity so that your end date is not impacted by the raised risks.
options.C: Gather risk mitigation responses from your retrospectives and assign them to an owner.
options.D: Place at least one risk response in each Sprint in an agile project to manage and close.

explanation: A risk adjusted backlog is a backlog that includes work and actions to address threats and opportunities. Risk responses are placed into the backlog, prioritized alongside other work, and assigned to Sprints to be worked on and closed. Page 185, risk adjusted backlog, PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You are delivering a project to a functional area in your organization. The project is highly complex and there are many risks to project delivery. The functional manager would like to see the biggest risks that are assigned to her. What will you do next?

options.A: Show the risk adjusted backlog.
options.B: Show the risk register.
options.C: Show the risk management plan.
options.D: Show her the qualitative risk assessment.

explanation: A risk register is a repository in which outputs of risk management processes are recorded, including the person responsible for managing each risk, the probability and impact, planned risk responses, and more. Page 185, risk register, PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: Your project has been in progress for the past 6 months. Someone you have not met before approaches you and says they have not been engaged sufficiently, even though he believes his team is highly impacted by the change. He advises he may block the project as a result. What are you going to do next?

options.A: Perform a stakeholder engagement assessment with your stakeholder.
options.B: Add him to the stakeholder register including his salary and interest information.
options.C: Add him to the stakeholder register including his impact and influence information.
options.D: Raise a risk to the project delivery due to the nature of his actions.

explanation: A stakeholder register records information about project stakeholders, including an assessment and classification of stakeholders. Ensuring his details and position are recorded before taking further action is the most appropriate step. Page 185, stakeholder register, PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You are a product owner on an agile project and you have created a product backlog of features in a prioritized order for delivery. You ask the team to break down the first feature into user stories and estimate their effort. What will you do next?

options.A: Create an iteration plan with your team.
options.B: Create the product road map with your team.
options.C: Perform a Sprint review.
options.D: Begin daily stand-ups with your team.

explanation: An iteration plan is a detailed plan for the current iteration that usually matches the estimated work for the Sprint to the average Sprint velocity. Items from the product backlog are broken down into user stories and placed into upcoming Sprints. Page 186, iteration plan, PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You have worked with your project customer to create a list of features that will bring the most value to them. They would like to see when some of this value will be delivered and the expected outcomes of each. What will you do next?

options.A: Show the features at the next Sprint review so the customer can see what they look like.
options.B: Place the features on a release plan and show this to the customer.
options.C: Check the features against the velocity chart to see when they might be delivered.
options.D: Place the features into the next Sprint plan and show this to your customer.

explanation: A release plan sets expectations for the dates, features, and outcomes expected to be delivered over the course of multiple iterations, providing the forward view the customer needs. Page 186, release plan, PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You are in the planning stages of your project where you need to find out exactly what the project customer needs so you can find a solution and make it happen. Your business analysts are new to their roles and they are not sure what their process will be. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask the business analysts to begin writing user stories.
options.B: Create the project management plan to outline how the project will be delivered.
options.C: Work with the business analyst to create a requirements management plan.
options.D: Create a work breakdown structure on the project scope.

explanation: The requirements management plan is a component of the project management plan that describes how requirements will be analyzed, documented, and managed. Page 186, requirements management plan, PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You are working on a new software application with a lot of complex interdependencies. The senior user providing and testing most of the requirements is concerned that some of those dependencies will cause errors upon release if they are not checked thoroughly. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask the senior user for a written list of all his requirements then check if they're included in the project scope.
options.B: Raise a risk on the software complexity with the project team.
options.C: Create an affinity diagram with the senior user.
options.D: Review the test plan with the senior user.

explanation: A test plan is a document that describes the deliverables that will be tested, the tests that will be conducted, and the processes that will be used in testing. It forms the basis for formally testing components and deliverables, including complex interdependencies. Page 186, test plan, PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You are planning your project and have captured the requirements from the project customer. You ask your team to plan the project scope but they need to see exactly what needs to be delivered and how each part relates to each other. What will you do next?

options.A: Create a product breakdown structure with your team.
options.B: Create the requirements traceability matrix with your project team.
options.C: Create the risk breakdown structure with your project team.
options.D: Create the scope management plan with your project team.

explanation: A product breakdown structure is a hierarchical structure reflecting a product's components and deliverables, providing an exhaustive hierarchical tree structure of deliverables arranged in a whole-to-parts relationship. Page 187, product breakdown structure, PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You have just finished breaking down the scope with your project team that will meet your customer's requirements. Your program manager wishes to control the project scope so any changes go through the proper process. What will you do next?

options.A: Create the change management plan to see how changes will be managed.
options.B: Baseline the project scope and note it in the configuration management plan so that it is a baselined item.
options.C: Create the scope management plan with a rule minimizing changes.
options.D: Create a resource assignment matrix limiting the roles who can approve project scope changes.

explanation: A baseline is the approved version of a work product or plan where actual performance is compared to the baseline to identify variances. Baselined items are noted in the configuration management plan. Page 188, baselines, PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You have planned and begun the current Sprint where the project team have committed to delivering and releasing a particular feature. You need to see how the team are tracking towards their Sprint goal. What will you do next?

options.A: Review the kanban board.
options.B: Review the team velocity chart.
options.C: Review the Sprint burndown chart.
options.D: Perform a Sprint review with the team.

explanation: A burndown chart is a graphical representation of the work remaining in a time box such as a Sprint, comparing the ideal trend with the items actually completed. A Sprint review, by contrast, demonstrates what was created during the Sprint once the Sprint is complete. Page 188, burndown chart, PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: Your agile project has recently been merged into a larger program and the program manager is reviewing how the project is tracking. She asks you how much has been completed in design, but also how much has been completed in development, testing, and sign off. What will you do next?

options.A: Show her the Sprint Kanban board
options.B: Take her through the team product backlog
options.C: Show her the team cumulative flow diagram
options.D: Show her the team velocity chart

explanation: A cumulative flow diagram indicates features completed over time, separated at each stage such as features in the backlog, then design, development, testing, and more. It covers the whole project, not just a single Sprint. Page 188 under cumulative flow diagram in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: The features developed by your agile team have added some complexity to your website, delivered quickly with no time to refactor the code. You believe the user stories are moving slower now as a result, but you can't be sure. What will you do next?

options.A: Check the cycle time chart of the team
options.B: Shadow the developers to overview the work that they are doing
options.C: View the product road map for proposed release dates
options.D: Review the points in the Sprint backlog versus the last three Sprints velocity

explanation: A cycle time chart shows the average cycle time of work items completed over time; it can be a scatter diagram or a bar chart and tracks how long individual user stories take to complete. This directly reveals whether stories are slowing down. The cycle time measures from when work starts to when it finishes for each card.

question: You are working on an agile project with a full product road map for the next 6 months. Your project team are not sure what the next feature does or how they should turn it into user stories. What will you do next?

options.A: Review the scope baseline
options.B: Story mapping with your team
options.C: Sprint planning with your team
options.D: Perform a retrospective with your team

explanation: A story map is a visual model of all the features and functionality desired for a given product, created to give the project team a holistic view of what they are building and why. It can also break the feature down into user stories based on functionality. Page 190 under story map in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You are a program manager on a large-scale agile program where each project team has its own scrum master. You need to know the rate at which work is flowing through each team so you can see if there are any blockers or issues and whether they will remain on track. What will you do next?

options.A: Raise your query at scrum of scrums
options.B: Attend the Sprint review of each team at the end of each Sprint
options.C: Review the schedule management plan for managing the schedule
options.D: Review the team velocity charts

explanation: The velocity chart tracks the rate at which deliverables are produced, validated, and accepted within a predefined interval like a Sprint. It shows what was committed versus what was completed for each Sprint, indicating the rate of work across teams. Page 190 under velocity chart in the PMBOK Guide.

question: Your project has been executing for the past 5 months and due to an organizational change a new project sponsor has come on board. He asks where the project is up to and whether it is on track. You tell him but he wants more information. What will you do next?

options.A: Show him the signed project charter
options.B: Show him the project management plan including the schedule management plan
options.C: Review the status report and previous status reports with the sponsor
options.D: Invite him to the next Sprint review so he can see the progress made

explanation: The status report provides a report on the current status of the project; it may include information on progress since the last report and forecasts for cost and schedule performance in the future. Reviewing current and previous status reports shows the sponsor whether the project is on track. Page 190 under reports, status report in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: Your agile project needs a third-party vendor to help complete the work. The product owner has multiple features on their road map which may change over time, making the scope of your project uncertain. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask your product owner to fix the features in scope so the project is more certain
options.B: Create a Cost Plus incentive fee contract with your procurement team
options.C: Create a firm fixed price contract with your procurement team
options.D: Use the external vendor funding to create a project team in-house and complete the project

explanation: Cost-reimbursable contracts involve payments to the seller for actual costs incurred plus a fee representing seller profit. They are used when the scope is not well defined or is subject to frequent change, which matches this agile scenario with uncertain scope. Includes Cost Plus Award Fee, Fixed Fee, or Incentive Fee. Page 191 under cost reimbursable contracts in the PMBOK Guide.

question: Your project is releasing a number of upgrades to a commercial building over the next 12 months. Material costs have changed a lot recently. You need to budget for the materials required; however, the volume of materials may change significantly as the project evolves. What will you do next?

options.A: Create an indefinite delivery indefinite quantity (IDIQ) contract for the materials
options.B: Create a firm fixed price contract for the materials
options.C: Ensure a sole supplier for your materials
options.D: Ensure multiple suppliers for your materials

explanation: Indefinite delivery indefinite quantity (IDIQ) provides for an indefinite quantity of goods or services with a stated lower and upper limit within a fixed time period. This suits scenarios where the quantity can change significantly. Fixed price works better if quantities are known. Page 191 under agreements and contracts in the PMBOK Guide 7th edition.

question: You have completed a make-or-buy analysis and agreed to hire a third-party vendor to complete part of your product. You create the statement of work and have a list of acceptable sellers that will meet the source selection criteria. The work is not complex and they understand the scope. What will you do next?

options.A: Send a request for proposal
options.B: Send a request for information from the sellers
options.C: Send a request for quotation
options.D: Hold a bidder's conference to ensure all sellers are on the same page

explanation: Bid documents are used to request proposals from prospective sellers. Request for quotation is best when the work is straightforward and you simply need a price. Request for proposal is used when the item is complex; request for information is used when more information is needed; a bidder's conference ensures all sellers have enough information. Page 192 under bid documents in the PMBOK Guide.

question: Your project is new and the team have just come on board. There have been multiple disagreements and the work is slow as they try to understand where they fit in the project and the team. What will you do next?

options.A: Review the project charter
options.B: Review the definition of ready
options.C: Review the project management plan
options.D: Review the team charter

explanation: The project team charter records the team values, agreements, and operating guidelines. It establishes clear expectations regarding acceptable behavior by project team members, helping them understand where they fit in the project and the team. Page 192 under project team charter in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You have created a high-level view of the features your project will deliver and prioritized them in a prioritized product backlog. The development team is ready to start work but they don't know where to start as the information is too high level. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask the team to begin with the highest priority item first
options.B: Help the team break the features down into user stories to place in a Sprint
options.C: Hold a retrospective with the team to understand what is blocking them
options.D: Reprioritize the features into an order that makes sense for the developers

explanation: A user story is a brief description of an outcome for a specific user; it has enough information at a level which team members can work on that item. Breaking high-level features down into user stories gives the team actionable items they can place into a Sprint. Page 192 under other artifacts, user story in the PMBOK Guide.
